cards.spanishPhrase: ¡Hola! ¿Cómo estás hoy?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ hǎo! Nǐ jīntiān zěnme yàng?
cards.grammarNote:
你好 (nǐ hǎo) is the standard greeting, lit. 'you good.' More formal: 您好 (nín hǎo), using the honorific 您 for elders or strangers.
Among friends, 嗨 (hāi) or 你好吗 (nǐ hǎo ma, 'are you well?') are common.
The response to 你好 is simply 你好 back.
请 (qǐng) precedes a verb to make a polite request: 请坐 (please sit), 请问 (may I ask). For offering something: 请 alone. To ask for an item: 给我... 好吗? or just 来一个... (bring one...).
cards.spanishPhrase: Por favor, siéntate aquí.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng zuò zài zhèlǐ.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¡Muchas gracias por tu ayuda!
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Fēicháng xièxie nǐ de bāngzhù!
cards.grammarNote:
谢谢 (xièxie) is casual and universal.
More emphatic: 非常谢谢 (fēicháng xièxie, 'thank you very much') or 多谢 (duō xiè).
More formal/written: 感谢 (gǎnxiè).
To thank someone specifically: 谢谢你的帮助 (xièxie nǐ de bāngzhù, 'thank you for your help').
The common response is 不客气 (bù kèqi) or 没事 (méi shì).
想要 (xiǎng yào) = would like (softer, more polite). 要 (yào) = want/will (more direct, also used for future). In a restaurant, 我要... is common and perfectly natural. 想 alone = think/feel like doing.
cards.spanishPhrase: Quiero un vaso de agua, por favor.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiǎng yào yī bēi shuǐ, xièxie.

cards.spanishPhrase: Necesito un poco más de tiempo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xūyào duō yīdiǎn shíjiān.
cards.grammarNote:
我需要 (wǒ xūyào) follows the pattern Subject + Verb + Object, identical to English word order.
需要 means 'need' and never changes form regardless of who is speaking.
A softer alternative is 我想要 (wǒ xiǎng yào, 'I would like').
Drop 我 when it is obvious from context, e.g.
需要帮忙吗？ ('need help?').
我叫 (wǒ jiào) = my name is (lit. 'I am called'). More natural in conversation than 我的名字是. 我姓... (wǒ xìng...) = my surname is... — used when giving a last name in formal contexts.
cards.spanishPhrase: Me llamo Ana, mucho gusto.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我叫安娜，很高兴认识你。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jiào Ānnà, hěn gāoxìng rènshi nǐ.

cards.spanishPhrase: Yo vivo aquí con mi familia.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ hé jiārén zhù zài zhèlǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
我 (wǒ) is the first-person pronoun and never changes form — no conjugation, no case distinctions like yo/me/mí in Spanish.
Third tone (falling-rising).
Subjects are frequently dropped in Chinese when they are clear from context, so 去了 (qù le) alone can mean 'I went' or 'he went' depending on the situation.
我 doubles as 'me' and 'my' (add 的 for possession: 我的).
你 (nǐ) = you (everyday). 您 (nín) = you (formal/respectful) — used with elders, customers, or in formal settings. In most daily conversation, 你 is fine.
cards.spanishPhrase: Tú hablas español muy bien.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ zhōngwén shuō de hěn hǎo.

cards.spanishPhrase: Él trabaja en una tienda grande.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā zài yī jiā dà shāngdiàn gōngzuò.
cards.grammarNote:
他 (tā) means 'he/him' and is third tone.
Crucially, 他, 她 (she), and 它 (it) are all pronounced tā — they are only distinguished in writing.
Chinese verbs never conjugate for person: 他去 / 我去 / 你去 all use the same verb 去.
Pronouns are often dropped when the subject is established, so 他工作 could simply become 工作 in context.
他 (he) and 她 (she) are both pronounced tā — identical in speech, different only in writing. Context makes the meaning clear in conversation.
cards.spanishPhrase: Ella come arroz todos los días.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā měitiān dōu chī mǐfàn.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ello no importa mucho ahora.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā xiànzài bù tài zhòngyào.
cards.grammarNote:
它 (tā) refers to animals and inanimate objects, but in spoken Chinese all three pronouns (他/她/它) sound identical.
Chinese often avoids 'it' altogether — speakers just name the thing or leave the subject implied.
这个 (zhège, 'this one') or 那个 (nàge, 'that one') often substitute for 'it' in pointing situations.

cards.spanishPhrase: Nosotros vamos al mercado juntos.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen yīqǐ qù shìchǎng.
cards.grammarNote:
我们 (wǒmen) = we/us, formed by adding 们 (men) to 我.
The 们 suffix pluralizes pronouns: 你们 (nǐmen, you all), 他们 (tāmen, they).
Note 们 is neutral tone.
Chinese does not distinguish 'we including you' vs. 'we excluding you' grammatically in everyday speech, though some dialects do.
As with all pronouns, drop 我们 when context is clear.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ellos llegan mañana por la tarde.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tāmen míngtiān xiàwǔ dào.
cards.grammarNote:
他们 (tāmen) = they (male or mixed group).
她们 (tāmen) = they (all female, same pronunciation).
它们 (tāmen) = they (animals/objects).
All three sound identical in speech.
们 is a neutral-tone suffix that only attaches to pronouns referring to people, not to nouns — you cannot say 朋友们 in a strictly grammatical sense, though it appears informally.
这 (zhè) = this. 这个 (zhège) = this one (with the measure word 个). In Chinese, a measure word (量词) goes between number/demonstrative and noun: 这本书 (this book — 本 is the measure word for books).
cards.spanishPhrase: Este libro es muy interesante.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhè běn shū hěn yǒu yìsi.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ese restaurante tiene buena comida.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nà jiā fànguǎn de cài hěn hǎochī.
cards.grammarNote:
那个 (nàge) = that one; 个 (ge) is the most common measure word and acts as a placeholder here.
When the noun follows, the measure word must match: 那家饭馆 (nà jiā fànguǎn, 'that restaurant' — 家 is the measure word for businesses).
Near = 这个 (zhège, 'this one'), far = 那个 (nàge, 'that one').
The 个 can be dropped before some nouns in casual speech: 那人 (nà rén, 'that person').

cards.spanishPhrase: Mi casa está cerca de aquí.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ de jiā zài zhèlǐ fùjìn.
cards.grammarNote:
我的 (wǒ de) = my/mine; 的 (de, neutral tone) marks possession like an apostrophe-s.
For close relationships (family, body parts), 的 is often dropped: 我妈妈 (wǒ māma, 'my mom'), 我手 (wǒ shǒu, 'my hand').
With less intimate nouns, keep 的: 我的书 (wǒ de shū, 'my book').
The possessive pattern is always Possessor + 的 + Noun.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Quién viene a la fiesta hoy?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān shéi lái cānjiā jùhuì?
cards.grammarNote:
谁 (shéi, also pronounced shuí) = who.
Crucially, question words in Chinese stay in the same position as the answer — they are not moved to the front as in Spanish (¿Quién...?).
今天谁来？ = 'Who is coming today?' (lit. 'Today who comes?').
你是谁？ = 'Who are you?' No inversion needed.
谁的 = 'whose.'

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Qué quieres comer esta noche?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ jīnwǎn xiǎng chī shénme?
cards.grammarNote:
什么 (shénme) = what.
Like all question words in Chinese, it stays in place: 你想吃什么？ (lit. 'You want eat what?') — the word order mirrors the answer 你想吃米饭.
什么 can modify nouns: 什么时候 (what time/when), 什么地方 (what place/where).
什么？ alone = 'What?' as a request to repeat.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Cuándo llega el próximo autobús?
cards.chinesePhrase: 下一班公共汽车什么时候来？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xià yī bān gōnggòng qìchē shénme shíhou lái?
cards.grammarNote:
什么时候 (shénme shíhou) = when, lit. 'what time.' Unlike Spanish ¿Cuándo...?, the phrase stays in place: 你什么时候来？ (lit. 'You when come?').
Time expressions in Chinese typically appear before the verb or at the start of the sentence: 他明天来 (tā míngtiān lái, 'he comes tomorrow').
几点 (jǐ diǎn) = 'what time (o'clock)' for clock times specifically.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Dónde está el baño, por favor?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xǐshǒujiān zài nǎlǐ?
cards.grammarNote:
在哪里 (zài nǎlǐ) = where, lit. 'at where.' 在 is a preposition/verb of location.
The question word stays in place: 洗手间在哪里？ = 'Where is the bathroom?' (lit. 'Bathroom at where?').
哪里 (nǎlǐ) and 哪儿 (nǎr, Beijing dialect) are interchangeable.
To say 'where are you going,' drop 在: 你去哪里？ or 你去哪儿？

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Por qué no viniste ayer?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ zuótiān wèishénme méi lái?
cards.grammarNote:
为什么 (wèishénme) = why, lit. 'for what reason.' It typically appears before the verb or after the subject: 你为什么没来？ = 'Why didn't you come?' (lit. 'You why not come?').
The answer uses 因为 (yīnwèi, because): 因为我生病了 (because I was sick).
为什么 can open a sentence: 为什么你不喜欢？ is also natural.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Cómo vas al trabajo cada día?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ měitiān zěnme qù shàngbān?
cards.grammarNote:
怎么 (zěnme) = how (manner/method).
It precedes the verb: 怎么去？ = 'How do you get there?' 怎么样 (zěnme yàng) = 'how about / how is it?' — used for opinions or conditions (今天怎么样？= 'How's today?').
多 (duō) + adjective = 'how + adjective': 多远？(how far?), 多长时间？(how long?).
早上好 (zǎoshang hǎo) = formal good morning. 早 (zǎo) = casual good morning. 你好 works any time of day. 下午好 (xiàwǔ hǎo) = good afternoon. 晚上好 (wǎnshang hǎo) = good evening.
cards.spanishPhrase: ¡Buenos días! ¿Dormiste bien?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zǎoshang hǎo! Nǐ shuì de hǎo ma?

cards.spanishPhrase: Estoy buscando mis llaves ahora.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiànzài zài zhǎo wǒ de yàoshi.
cards.grammarNote:
找 (zhǎo) = to look for/search.
The object follows directly: 找人 (find a person), 找钥匙 (look for keys).
Ongoing action is shown with 在 before the verb: 我在找钥匙 (I am looking for my keys).
找到 (zhǎo dào) = to find successfully — the 到 indicates completion or result.
找不到 = can't find it.

不好意思 (bù hǎo yìsi) = excuse me / I'm embarrassed to ask — the most natural all-purpose attention-getter. 打扰一下 (dǎrǎo yīxià) = 'sorry to interrupt'. 请问 (qǐng wèn) = 'may I ask' — great before a question.
cards.spanishPhrase: Perdón, ¿puede repetir eso?
cards.chinesePhrase: 不好意思，你能再说一遍吗？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Bù hǎo yìsi, nǐ néng zài shuō yī biàn ma?

cards.grammarNote: 能 (néng) = can (physical ability or circumstance): 我能游泳 (I can swim). 可以 (kěyǐ) = may/can (permission or possibility): 我可以进来吗? (May I come in?). 会 (huì) = can (learned skill): 我会说中文 (I can speak Chinese).
cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Puedes venir mañana a las diez?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ míngtiān shí diǎn néng lái ma?

cards.spanishPhrase: No sé dónde está la tienda.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ bù zhīdào shāngdiàn zài nǎlǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
我不知道 (wǒ bù zhīdào) = I don't know.
不 (bù, fourth tone) negates most verbs and adjectives.
知道 (zhīdào) = to know a fact; different from 认识 (rènshi) = to know a person.
A shorter casual response: 不知道 (drop 我).
我不确定 (wǒ bù quèdìng) = 'I'm not sure' is a softer alternative.
有 (yǒu) = have or there is/are. 我有钱 (I have money). 这里有咖啡 (There is coffee here). Negated: 没有 (méiyǒu) — note: never 不有, always 没有. 没有 alone = 'I don't have it / there isn't any'.
cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Tienes un bolígrafo que me prestes?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ yǒu bǐ kěyǐ jiè gěi wǒ ma?

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Qué haces el fin de semana?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ zhōumò zuò shénme?
cards.grammarNote:
做 (zuò) = to do/make, used for activities and producing things: 做饭 (cook food), 做作业 (do homework), 做生意 (do business).
干 (gàn) is a more colloquial synonym.
做什么 = 'do what / what are you doing?' The pattern Subject + 做 + Object mirrors English.
Unlike Spanish hacer, 做 does not change form.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me gusta comer con mi familia.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我喜欢和家人一起吃饭。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xǐhuān hé jiārén yīqǐ chīfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
吃 (chī) = to eat.
The general phrase 吃饭 (chī fàn, lit. 'eat rice') means 'to have a meal' and is used regardless of what is eaten.
你吃了吗？ (Have you eaten?) is a common casual greeting.
吃 can be followed by a specific food: 吃面 (eat noodles).
No conjugation — the same form works for all persons and tenses.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me gusta mucho la comida china.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ hěn xǐhuān Zhōngguó cài.
cards.grammarNote:
喜欢 (xǐhuān) = to like, and it directly precedes what is liked: 我喜欢咖啡 (I like coffee) — the structure is simpler than Spanish gustar where the subject and object flip.
很喜欢 (hěn xǐhuān) = like a lot; 不喜欢 = don't like.
喜欢 + verb = like doing something: 我喜欢跑步 (I like running).
爱 (ài) = love, a stronger version.

cards.spanishPhrase: Quiero ir, pero estoy muy cansado.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiǎng qù, dànshì wǒ hěn lèi.
cards.grammarNote:
但是 (dànshì) = but/however, used to contrast two clauses.
Often paired with 虽然 (suīrán, although): 虽然很累，但是我去了 (Although tired, I still went).
In casual speech, 但 alone works.
可是 (kěshì) is a close synonym, slightly more spoken.
Word order is the same as English: Clause A + 但是 + Clause B.

和 (hé) = and, connecting nouns: 水和食物 (water and food). 还有 (háiyǒu) = also/and also, adding items: 还有一件事 (and there's one more thing). 和 does not connect verbs or sentences — use 然后 (ránhòu) for 'and then'.
cards.spanishPhrase: Quiero pan y leche, por favor.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ yào miànbāo hé niúnǎi, xièxie.

cards.grammarNote: 还是 (háishì) = or (in questions): 你要茶还是咖啡？(Tea or coffee?). 或者 (huòzhě) = or (in statements): 你可以喝茶或者咖啡 (You can have tea or coffee). A common mistake is swapping them.
cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Quieres té o café esta mañana?
cards.chinesePhrase: 你今天早上要茶还是咖啡？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ jīntiān zǎoshang yào chá háishì kāfēi?

cards.grammarNote: 了 (le) after an adjective or verb marks a change of state: 饿了 = have become hungry, 渴了 = have become thirsty. 我很饿 = I am hungry (general state). 我饿了 = I've gotten hungry (new state). Both are common.
cards.spanishPhrase: Tengo hambre, ¿podemos comer ya?
cards.chinesePhrase: 我饿了，我们可以吃饭了吗？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ è le, wǒmen kěyǐ chīfàn le ma?

cards.spanishPhrase: Tengo sed, necesito agua ahora.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ kě le, wǒ xiànzài xūyào shuǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
渴了 (kě le) = thirsty now, lit. 'thirsty (change of state).' Chinese expresses 'to be thirsty/hungry' as adjectives, not with tener like Spanish — no verb 'to have' needed.
了 (le) signals a new state: 我渴了 = 'I've become thirsty.' Similarly: 饿了 (è le, hungry), 累了 (lèi le, tired).
The full sentence is 我渴了 (wǒ kě le).

cards.spanishPhrase: Esa persona habla tres idiomas.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nàge rén huì shuō sān zhǒng yǔyán.
cards.grammarNote:
人 (rén) = person/people.
It does not change for singular or plural — context or numbers clarify: 一个人 (yī gè rén, one person), 很多人 (hěn duō rén, many people).
人 is also a suffix for nationalities and occupations: 中国人 (Zhōngguó rén, Chinese person), 工人 (gōngrén, worker).
The measure word for people is 个 (gè) or 位 (wèi, polite).

cards.spanishPhrase: Este lugar es muy tranquilo y bonito.
cards.chinesePhrase: 这个地方很安静，很漂亮。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhège dìfāng hěn ānjìng, hěn piàoliang.
cards.grammarNote:
地方 (dìfāng) = place/location, a general term.
More specific: 地点 (dìdiǎn) for a designated spot, 位置 (wèizhi) for position/seat.
这个地方 (zhège dìfāng) = this place.
In casual speech, 地方 can be shortened: 这地方.
Location words like 这里/那里 can substitute for 地方 without the measure word 个.
东西 (dōngxi) = a physical thing or stuff. 事情 (shìqing) = a matter or situation. 买东西 (buy things / go shopping). 有事情 (have something going on / there's a matter). Don't mix them up.
cards.spanishPhrase: Hay muchas cosas en esta tienda.
cards.chinesePhrase: 这家商店里有很多东西。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhè jiā shāngdiàn lǐ yǒu hěn duō dōngxi.

cards.spanishPhrase: Por favor, ven aquí un momento.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng guòlái zhèlǐ yīxià.
cards.grammarNote:
这里 (zhèlǐ) = here, lit. 'this inside/place.' Interchangeable with 这儿 (zhèr) in Beijing/northern dialect.
这里 follows location verbs: 在这里 (at here), 来这里 (come here), 到这里 (arrive here).
The 这/那 distinction mirrors Spanish este/ese/aquel to some extent: 这 = near, 那 = far.
No grammatical gender or case to worry about.

cards.spanishPhrase: El baño está allí, al fondo.
cards.chinesePhrase: 洗手间在那里，在最里面。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xǐshǒujiān zài nàlǐ, zài zuì lǐmiàn.
cards.grammarNote:
那里 (nàlǐ) = there, lit. 'that place.' Interchangeable with 那儿 (nàr) colloquially.
在那里 = over there (location); 去那里 = go there (direction).
Chinese uses 这/那 for a simple near/far distinction, unlike Spanish este/ese/aquel (three levels).
哪里 (nǎlǐ, with a question tone) = where? — same sound base, different tone.

很 (hěn) = very, but in 'A是B' sentences it's often required even without emphasis: 我很好 (I'm good) — dropping 很 makes it sound like a contrast. 非常 (fēicháng) = extremely. 太 (tài) = too/so: 太好了！(So great!)
cards.spanishPhrase: La comida aquí es muy rica.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhèlǐ de shíwù hěn hǎochī.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¡Adiós! Hasta la próxima vez.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zàijiàn! Xià cì jiàn.
cards.grammarNote:
再见 (zàijiàn) = goodbye, lit. 'again see' — similar in spirit to Spanish 'hasta la vista.' Casual alternatives: 拜拜 (bāibāi, from English 'bye-bye') or just 拜 (bāi).
回头见 (huítóu jiàn) = 'see you later' (lit. 'turn head see').
明天见 (míngtiān jiàn) = see you tomorrow.
见 (jiàn) alone means 'to see/meet,' so you can swap in any time word.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Puedo pedir un vaso de agua?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ kěyǐ yào yī bēi shuǐ ma?
cards.grammarNote:
水 (shuǐ) = water.
To request it, use a measure word: 一杯水 (yī bēi shuǐ, one glass/cup of water), 一瓶水 (yī píng shuǐ, one bottle of water).
Chinese requires a measure word between a number and a noun — you cannot say 一水.
热水 (rè shuǐ) = hot water, 冷水 (lěng shuǐ) = cold water, 开水 (kāi shuǐ) = boiled water (very common in China).

cards.spanishPhrase: La comida del restaurante es deliciosa.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Fànguǎn de shíwù hěn hǎochī.
cards.grammarNote:
食物 (shíwù) is the general/formal word for food, more common in writing.
In everyday speech, 吃的 (chī de, lit. 'stuff to eat') or 菜 (cài, dishes/food) are more natural.
饭 (fàn) = cooked rice but also 'meal' broadly: 吃饭 = to eat a meal.
好吃 (hào chī) = delicious, lit. 'good to eat' — a very common food compliment.
家 (jiā) = home (warm, where you belong): 回家 (go home), 我家 (my home). 房子 (fángzi) = house (the physical building). 在家 = at home. 家 also means 'family': 我家有四口人 (My family has four people).
cards.spanishPhrase: Voy a casa después del trabajo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiàbān yǐhòu huí jiā.

cards.spanishPhrase: La tienda abre a las nueve.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Shāngdiàn jiǔ diǎn kāimén.
cards.grammarNote:
商店 (shāngdiàn) = store/shop.
The measure word for stores and businesses is 家 (jiā): 一家商店 (one store), 那家店 (that shop).
More specific terms: 超市 (chāoshì, supermarket), 书店 (shūdiàn, bookstore).
店 (diàn) alone = shop, and it combines productively: 咖啡店 (coffee shop), 饭店 (restaurant/hotel depending on context).

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Necesitas ayuda con eso?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ xūyào bāngzhù ma?
cards.grammarNote:
帮助 (bāngzhù) is the noun/formal verb for 'help.' The colloquial verb is 帮 (bāng): 帮我 (help me), 帮个忙 (do a favor).
你能帮我吗？ uses the modal 能 (néng, can/able to).
As a noun: 需要帮助吗？ (Do you need help?).
帮 can also mean 'help someone do something': 帮我找 (help me look for).

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Dónde está el baño, por favor?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐngwèn xǐshǒujiān zài nǎlǐ?
cards.grammarNote:
洗手间 (xǐshǒujiān) = bathroom/restroom, lit. 'wash-hands-room' — the polite public term.
Alternatives: 卫生间 (wèishēngjiān, sanitary room — common in mainland China), 厕所 (cèsuǒ, toilet/WC — more blunt), 浴室 (yùshì, bathroom with bathing facilities).
In Taiwan, 化妆室 (huàzhuāngshì) is used.
For public signs, look for 男 (nán, men) and 女 (nǚ, women).

cards.spanishPhrase: Siempre desayuno antes de salir.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zǒngshì chūmén qián chī zǎofàn.
cards.grammarNote:
总是 (zǒngshì) = always, and it goes before the verb: 我总是迟到 (I always arrive late).
Adverbs of frequency in Chinese precede the verb, not after it as sometimes in Spanish.
Related: 经常 (jīngcháng, often), 有时候 (yǒu shíhou, sometimes), 从不 (cóng bù, never).
总 alone can mean 'always/constantly' in spoken contexts.
从不 (cóngbù) = never (habitual). 从来不 (cónglái bù) = have never. 从来没有 (cónglái méiyǒu) = have never done: 我从来没有去过日本 (I've never been to Japan). All three place the negative before the verb.
cards.spanishPhrase: Nunca como carne por la mañana.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zǎoshang cóngbù chī ròu.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Quieres ir al parque conmigo?
cards.chinesePhrase: 你想和我一起去公园吗？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ xiǎng hé wǒ yīqǐ qù gōngyuán ma?
cards.grammarNote:
去 (qù) = to go (away from speaker).
The opposite is 来 (lái, to come, toward speaker).
These direction verbs attach to other verbs: 走过去 (walk over there), 跑过来 (run over here).
Destination follows 去 directly: 去学校 (go to school) — no preposition needed, unlike Spanish ir a.
去 is also used with activities: 去买东西 (go shopping).

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Puedes venir a mi casa mañana?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ míngtiān néng lái wǒ jiā ma?
cards.grammarNote:
来 (lái) = to come (toward speaker or a reference point).
Contrast with 去 (qù, go away).
来 attaches after verbs to show movement toward: 送来 (deliver/bring here), 拿来 (bring it here).
To invite someone: 来我家 (come to my place).
能来吗？ (Can you come?) uses modal 能.
来 is also used as a filler to introduce an action: 来，我们开始吧 (Come on, let's start).

cards.spanishPhrase: No entiendo esta palabra en chino.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ bù míngbai zhège zhōngwén cí.
cards.grammarNote:
明白 (míngbai) = to understand/grasp clearly, often used after an explanation.
懂 (dǒng) is a close synonym: 你听懂了吗？ (Did you understand what you heard?).
明白了 (míngbai le) = 'Got it / I understand now' — the 了 marks the new state of understanding.
我不明白 and 我不懂 are both natural ways to say 'I don't understand.'

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Puedes hablar más despacio, por favor?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ néng shuō màn yīdiǎn ma?
cards.grammarNote:
说 (shuō) = to speak/say.
说话 (shuō huà) = to speak/talk (in general).
说中文 (shuō Zhōngwén) = to speak Chinese.
To ask someone to slow down: 说慢一点 (speak a bit slower) — the adverb 慢一点 follows the verb.
讲 (jiǎng) is a synonym often used for 'to explain' or 'to tell a story.' 聊 (liáo) = to chat casually.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Me puedes dar un poco de sal?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ néng gěi wǒ yīdiǎn yán ma?
cards.grammarNote:
给 (gěi) = to give, and also acts as a preposition meaning 'to/for': 给我 (give me / for me).
Double-object pattern: 给我一点盐 (give me some salt) = Verb + Recipient + Thing, same as English.
给 before a verb means 'for someone's benefit': 我给你买了 (I bought it for you).
As a preposition it can also mean 'let': 给我看看 (let me see).
看 (kàn) = to look at / watch / read. 见 (jiàn) = to see / meet. 看书 (read a book), 看电视 (watch TV). 见面 (meet up). 我看不见 = I can't see it (visibility blocked). 看不到 = can't see it (too far/hidden).
cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Ves esa persona allí?
cards.chinesePhrase: 你看到那里那个人了吗？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ kàndào nàlǐ nàge rén le ma?

cards.spanishPhrase: Fui al cine con mi amigo.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我和朋友一起去看电影了。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ hé péngyou yīqǐ qù kàn diànyǐng le.
cards.grammarNote:
和 (hé) = and/with.
When connecting nouns: 我和你 (you and I).
When meaning 'together with,' add 一起 (yīqǐ): 我和朋友一起去 (I went with a friend, lit. 'I and friend together go').
跟 (gēn) is a synonym for 'with' in this sense: 我跟你去 (I'll go with you).
Note: 和 does not connect verbs or sentences the way 'and' does in English — use 然后 (ránhòu, then) for sequential actions.

cards.spanishPhrase: No puedo dormir sin música.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Méiyǒu yīnyuè wǒ shuì bù zháo.
cards.grammarNote:
没有 (méiyǒu) = without/don't have, lit. 'not have.' The construction is 没有 + Noun: 没有音乐 (without music).
没有 is also the negation of 有 (to have): 我没有钱 (I don't have money).
For 'without doing something,' use 不 + Verb: 不说话 (without speaking).
Note that 不 negates present/future, while 没 negates past or existence.

cards.spanishPhrase: Estudio chino para viajar a China.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我为了去中国而学中文。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ wèile qù Zhōngguó ér xué zhōngwén.
cards.grammarNote:
为了 (wèile) = for the purpose of / in order to, expressing purpose or motivation.
It goes before the verb phrase: 为了学中文，我每天练习 (In order to learn Chinese, I practice every day).
For beneficiary ('for someone'), use 为 (wèi) or 给 (gěi): 我为你做的 (I did it for you).
为了 is more formal/emphatic; casual speech may just use context.
在 (zài) = at/in/on. It covers location: 在家 (at home), 在桌子上 (on the table). For 'on top of': 在…上 (shàng). For 'inside': 在…里 (lǐ). For 'under': 在…下 (xià). The position word follows the place noun.
cards.spanishPhrase: El libro está en la mesa.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Shū zài zhuōzi shàng.

cards.spanishPhrase: Este hotel es muy bueno y barato.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhè jiā jiǔdiàn yòu hǎo yòu piányí.
cards.grammarNote:
好 (hǎo) = good, and adjectives in Chinese act directly as predicates without a linking verb: 这个好 (this is good) — no 是 needed.
很好 (hěn hǎo, very good) — 很 is required before adjectives in neutral sentences to avoid an implied comparison.
好 is also a verb complement meaning 'done/ready': 做好了 (finished doing it).
好 in a response = 'OK/alright.'

cards.spanishPhrase: El tiempo de hoy está muy malo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān de tiānqì hěn bù hǎo.
cards.grammarNote:
不好 (bù hǎo) = not good/bad, lit. 'not good.' Note 不 changes from fourth tone to second tone before another fourth-tone syllable: 不好 (bù hǎo stays as-is since 好 is third tone).
Dedicated words for bad: 坏 (huài, broken/morally bad), 差 (chà, poor quality).
天气不好 is natural; 天气很坏 sounds odd — use 天气很差 or 天气不好 instead.

cards.spanishPhrase: Mi apartamento es pequeño pero cómodo.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我的公寓很小，但是很舒服。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ de gōngyù hěn xiǎo, dànshì hěn shūfu.
cards.grammarNote:
小 (xiǎo) = small/little.
Adjectives precede nouns: 小房间 (small room), or serve as predicates: 房间很小 (the room is very small).
小 combines with other characters: 小心 (xiǎoxīn, be careful, lit. 'small heart'), 小姐 (xiǎojiě, miss/young woman).
Opposite: 大 (dà, big).
小 is also an affectionate prefix for names: 小李 (Xiao Li) = young or familiar Li.

cards.spanishPhrase: Hay mucha gente en el mercado.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Shìchǎng lǐ yǒu hěn duō rén.
cards.grammarNote:
很多 (hěn duō) = a lot/many, used before nouns: 很多人 (many people), 很多钱 (a lot of money).
多 (duō) alone = 'more/much' and can follow verbs: 多吃点 (eat more).
许多 (xǔduō) is a more formal synonym.
很多 does not require a measure word between it and a noun.
Opposite: 很少 (hěn shǎo, very few/little).

cards.spanishPhrase: Habla un poco más despacio, por favor.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng shuō màn yīdiǎnr.
cards.grammarNote:
一点 (yīdiǎn) = a little, used after verbs or adjectives: 慢一点 (a bit slower), 多一点 (a bit more).
Before nouns, use 一点儿 (yīdiǎnr, Beijing dialect with 儿) or 一些 (yīxiē, some): 一点儿水 (a little water).
有一点 (yǒu yīdiǎn) before an adjective = 'a bit [adjective]': 有一点贵 (a bit expensive).
The 儿 ending is optional outside northern dialects.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Cómo se dice 'manzana' en chino?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: 'Píngguǒ' yòng Xībānyáyǔ zěnme shuō?
cards.grammarNote:
___怎么说？(zěnme shuō) = How do you say ___?, lit. '[blank] how say?' The item you want to translate goes at the front.
用西班牙语怎么说？ = 'How do you say it in Spanish?' (用 yòng = using).
You can also ask: 这个用中文怎么说？ (How do you say this in Chinese?).
怎么写？(zěnme xiě?) = How do you write it?

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Puedes ayudarme a encontrar la salida?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ néng bāng wǒ zhǎodào chūkǒu ma?
cards.grammarNote:
你能帮我吗？(Nǐ néng bāng wǒ ma?) uses the modal 能 (néng, can/be able to).
能 and 可以 (kěyǐ) both express ability/permission but 能 often stresses physical capability while 可以 stresses permission: 你可以帮我吗？ is equally natural here.
Questions are formed by adding 吗 (ma) to the end — no inversion needed.
帮我 = 'help me'; 帮 can be followed by a specific task: 帮我找.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Cuánto cuesta esta camisa?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhè jiàn chènshān duōshǎo qián?
cards.grammarNote:
多少钱？(Duōshǎo qián?) = How much does it cost?, lit. 'how much money?' 多少 (duōshǎo) asks about quantity and can precede any noun: 多少人？(how many people?).
For small numbers (under 10), 几 (jǐ) is preferred: 几个人？(how many people?).
The full question 这个多少钱？needs no verb — 钱 (money) effectively communicates the meaning.
Prices: 块 (kuài) = yuan colloquially, 元 (yuán) formally.
对不起 (duìbuqǐ) = I'm sorry (genuine apology — lit. 'I can't face you'). 不好意思 (bù hǎo yìsi) = excuse me / I'm embarrassed — lighter, more social. 抱歉 (bàoqiàn) = formal apology.
cards.spanishPhrase: Lo siento mucho, fue mi culpa.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Duìbuqǐ, shì wǒ de cuò.

cards.spanishPhrase: No hay problema, podemos intentarlo mañana.
cards.chinesePhrase: 没问题，我们明天可以试试。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Méi wèntí, wǒmen míngtiān kěyǐ shìshi.
cards.grammarNote:
没问题 (méi wèntí) = no problem, lit. 'not have problem.' 没 negates 有-type sentences (existence/possession).
This is a very common reassurance, equivalent to 'sure, no worries.' Similar expressions: 没事 (méi shì, it's nothing/no worries), 没关系 (méi guānxi, it doesn't matter/that's OK — more often used to accept an apology).
All three start with 没 and are essential everyday phrases.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Me traes un vaso de agua?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Néng gěi wǒ ná yī bēi shuǐ ma?
cards.grammarNote:
水 (shuǐ) is the basic word for water, both drinking water and water in general.
Order with 来一杯水 (lái yī bēi shuǐ, bring a glass of water) or 我要一瓶水 (wǒ yào yī píng shuǐ, I'll have a bottle of water) — 杯 (bēi) is the measure word for cups/glasses, 瓶 (píng) for bottles.
Hot water is 热水 (rèshuǐ), cold water is 冷水 (lěngshuǐ).
In China it is common to be offered hot water (热水) by default.

cards.spanishPhrase: Como pan con mantequilla cada mañana.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我每天早上吃面包和黄油。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ měitiān zǎoshang chī miànbāo hé huángyóu.
cards.grammarNote:
面包 (miànbāo) lit. 'noodle-bun' — a quirky compound since bread was introduced after 面 (miàn, flour/noodle) was already in use; 包 (bāo) means bun or wrapped item.
Count individual loaves or rolls with 个: 一个面包 (one bread/loaf).
A slice is 一片面包 (yī piàn miànbāo), where 片 (piàn) is the measure word for flat, thin pieces.
Bread is less central to Chinese cuisine than Western food, so it appears mainly at breakfast or in Western-style cafes.

cards.spanishPhrase: El arroz frito me gusta mucho.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ hěn xǐhuān chī chǎo mǐfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
米饭 (mǐfàn) lit. 'rice-rice' — 米 (mǐ) = uncooked rice grains, 饭 (fàn) = cooked food/meal; together they specifically mean cooked rice as a dish.
碗 (wǎn) is the measure word: 来一碗米饭 (lái yī wǎn mǐfàn, bring a bowl of rice).
Fried rice is 炒米饭 (chǎo mǐfàn) or just 炒饭 (chǎofàn).
Rice is the staple of southern Chinese cuisine; in the north, wheat-based foods like noodles and steamed buns dominate.

cards.spanishPhrase: Bebo un vaso de leche cada día.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ měitiān hē yī bēi niúnǎi.
cards.grammarNote:
牛奶 (niúnǎi) lit. 'cow-milk' — 牛 (niú) = cow/ox, 奶 (nǎi) = milk/dairy.
杯 is the measure word for glasses: 一杯牛奶 (yī bēi niúnǎi); 瓶 for bottles: 一瓶牛奶 (yī píng niúnǎi).
Goat's milk is 羊奶 (yángnǎi, goat-milk) and soy milk — very popular in China — is 豆浆 (dòujiāng).
Dairy consumption has grown in China but is historically lower than in Western diets.

cards.spanishPhrase: Quiero dos huevos revueltos, por favor.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我想要两个炒鸡蛋，谢谢。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiǎng yào liǎng gè chǎo jīdàn, xièxie.
cards.grammarNote:
鸡蛋 (jīdàn) lit. 'chicken-egg' — 鸡 (jī) = chicken, 蛋 (dàn) = egg.
Count with 个: 一个鸡蛋 (yī gè jīdàn, one egg).
炒鸡蛋 (chǎo jīdàn) = scrambled/stir-fried eggs, 煮鸡蛋 (zhǔ jīdàn) = boiled egg, 荷包蛋 (hébāodàn) = poached egg.
The famous dish 西红柿炒鸡蛋 (stir-fried tomato and egg) is arguably China's most beloved home-cooked dish.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Puedo añadir queso a mi pizza?
cards.chinesePhrase: 我可以在披萨上加奶酪吗？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ kěyǐ zài pīsà shàng jiā nǎilào ma?
cards.grammarNote:
奶酪 (nǎilào) lit. 'milk-fermented' — 奶 (nǎi) = milk/dairy, 酪 (lào) = fermented dairy product.
Count with 块 (kuài, piece/block): 一块奶酪 (yī kuài nǎilào, a piece of cheese).
Cheese is not traditional in Chinese cuisine and is mainly encountered in Western-style restaurants or pizza; asking 有没有奶酪？ (yǒu méiyǒu nǎilào?, do you have cheese?) is useful in mixed-menu restaurants.

cards.spanishPhrase: El pollo a la plancha es delicioso.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Kǎo jīròu hěn hǎochī.
cards.grammarNote:
鸡肉 (jīròu) lit. 'chicken-meat' — 鸡 (jī) = chicken (the animal), 肉 (ròu) = meat/flesh.
When 肉 is added to an animal name it specifies the meat: 鸡肉 (chicken meat), 猪肉 (pork), 牛肉 (beef).
Order with 我要一份鸡肉 (wǒ yào yī fèn jīròu, I'll have a portion of chicken) — 份 (fèn) is the measure word for servings.
烤鸡 (kǎojī) = roast chicken, 炸鸡 (zhájī) = fried chicken.

cards.spanishPhrase: No como mucha carne roja.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ bù tài chī hóng ròu.
cards.grammarNote:
肉 (ròu) is the general word for meat or flesh.
In everyday Chinese, 肉 alone often implies pork by default, since pork is the most commonly eaten meat in China.
牛肉 (niúròu) = beef, 羊肉 (yángròu) = lamb/mutton, 猪肉 (zhūròu) = pork.
Order 我要一盘肉 (wǒ yào yī pán ròu, I'll have a plate of meat) — 盘 (pán) is the measure word for plates of food.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me gusta mucho comer pescado fresco.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ hěn xǐhuān chī xīnxiān de yú.
cards.grammarNote:
鱼 (yú) means both 'fish' (the animal) and fish as food — no separate word is needed.
Count whole fish with 条 (tiáo, a measure word for long flexible things): 一条鱼 (yī tiáo yú).
For a dish of fish at a restaurant use 盘: 来一盘鱼 (lái yī pán yú).
Fish is central to Chinese cuisine; a whole fish on the table symbolizes prosperity and completeness at celebratory meals.

cards.spanishPhrase: El cerdo con verduras es muy rico.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhūròu chǎo shūcài hěn hǎochī.
cards.grammarNote:
猪肉 (zhūròu) lit. 'pig-meat' — 猪 (zhū) = pig, 肉 (ròu) = meat.
Pork is the most consumed meat in China, so much so that 肉 alone often implies pork in context.
Classic pork dishes include 红烧肉 (hóngshāoròu, red-braised pork belly) and 回锅肉 (huíguōròu, twice-cooked pork).
Order with 份 (fèn, serving) or 盘 (pán, plate): 来一盘猪肉炒蔬菜 (bring a plate of stir-fried pork and vegetables).

cards.spanishPhrase: Como fruta fresca todos los días.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ měitiān dōu chī xīnxiān shuǐguǒ.
cards.grammarNote:
水果 (shuǐguǒ) lit. 'water-fruit' — 水 (shuǐ) = water, 果 (guǒ) = fruit/result; the compound evokes juicy, water-rich fruits.
斤 (jīn, half-kilogram) is the typical market measure: 一斤苹果 (yī jīn píngguǒ, half a kilo of apples).
At a restaurant you can order 来一份水果 (lái yī fèn shuǐguǒ, bring a fruit platter).
Fresh fruit is often served at the end of a meal in Chinese restaurants as a complimentary gesture.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Quieres una manzana roja o verde?
cards.chinesePhrase: 你想要红苹果还是绿苹果？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ xiǎng yào hóng píngguǒ háishì lǜ píngguǒ?
cards.grammarNote:
苹果 (píngguǒ) uses 果 (guǒ, fruit) as its second character; 苹 (píng) was chosen partly for sound.
Count with 个 (gè): 一个苹果 (yī gè píngguǒ, one apple).
Buy by weight: 来两斤苹果 (lái liǎng jīn píngguǒ, give me one kilogram of apples).
Incidentally, 苹果 is also the Chinese name for Apple Inc., so context usually makes the meaning clear.

cards.spanishPhrase: Los plátanos son baratos y nutritivos.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xiāngjiāo yòu piányí yòu yǒu yíngyǎng.
cards.grammarNote:
香蕉 (xiāngjiāo) lit. 'fragrant-plantain' — 香 (xiāng) = fragrant/aromatic, 蕉 (jiāo) = a type of plantain plant.
Count with 根 (gēn, a measure word for long thin things) or 个: 一根香蕉 (yī gēn xiāngjiāo, one banana).
Buy by weight at markets: 来一斤香蕉 (lái yī jīn xiāngjiāo, give me half a kilo of bananas).
Bananas are a very common and affordable fruit throughout China.

cards.spanishPhrase: Bebo jugo de naranja cada mañana.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ měitiān zǎoshang hē chéngzhī.
cards.grammarNote:
橙子 (chéngzi) — 橙 (chéng) is the character for the orange tree and its fruit, with 子 (zi) a neutral noun suffix.
Count with 个: 一个橙子 (yī gè chéngzi, one orange).
Orange juice is 橙汁 (chéngzhī).
Note that 橘子 (júzi, tangerine/mandarin orange) is more common in China than the larger navel orange; in casual speech the two are sometimes used interchangeably.

cards.spanishPhrase: Debes comer más verduras cada día.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ měitiān yīnggāi duō chī shūcài.
cards.grammarNote:
蔬菜 (shūcài) is the formal/general term for vegetables — 蔬 (shū) = vegetables/greens, 菜 (cài) = dish/vegetable/food.
In everyday speech 菜 alone often means vegetable or dish.
For a plate at a restaurant: 来一盘蔬菜 (lái yī pán shūcài, bring a plate of vegetables) — 盘 (pán) is the measure word.
炒蔬菜 (chǎo shūcài, stir-fried vegetables) is a standard side dish in Chinese restaurants.

cards.spanishPhrase: Las papas fritas me gustan mucho.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ hěn xǐhuān chī zhá tǔdòu.
cards.grammarNote:
土豆 (tǔdòu) lit. 'earth-bean' — 土 (tǔ) = soil/earth, 豆 (dòu) = bean/legume; potatoes grow underground like beans.
A more formal name is 马铃薯 (mǎlíngshǔ), used in written/scientific contexts.
Count with 个: 一个土豆 (yī gè tǔdòu).
A beloved dish is 土豆丝 (tǔdòusī, shredded potato stir-fry); French fries are 炸薯条 (zhá shǔtiáo).
Buy by 斤 at the market.

cards.spanishPhrase: El tomate con huevo es un plato chino.
cards.chinesePhrase: 西红柿炒鸡蛋是一道中国菜。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xīhóngshì chǎo jīdàn shì yī dào Zhōngguó cài.
cards.grammarNote:
西红柿 (xīhóngshì) lit. 'western-red-persimmon' — 西 (xī) = western, 红 (hóng) = red, 柿 (shì) = persimmon; when tomatoes arrived from the West they resembled persimmons in shape.
An alternate name is 番茄 (fānqié), more common in southern China and formal writing.
Count with 个: 两个西红柿 (two tomatoes).
西红柿炒鸡蛋 (stir-fried tomato and egg) is one of China's most iconic everyday dishes.

cards.spanishPhrase: Esta sopa tiene mucha cebolla.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhège tāng lǐ yǒu hěn duō yángcōng.
cards.grammarNote:
洋葱 (yángcōng) lit. 'foreign-onion' — 洋 (yáng) = foreign/western/ocean, 葱 (cōng) = scallion/green onion; the 洋 prefix indicates it came from abroad.
Count with 个: 一个洋葱 (yī gè yángcōng, one onion).
Green onions (葱, cōng) are a fundamental seasoning in Chinese cooking, used as a garnish on almost everything; 洋葱 specifically refers to the Western-style bulb onion.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ella come ensalada todos los días.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā měitiān dōu chī shālā.
cards.grammarNote:
沙拉 (shālā) is a phonetic loanword from English 'salad' — 沙 (shā, sand) and 拉 (lā, pull) were chosen purely for their sound and have no relevant meaning here.
Count portions with 份 (fèn): 一份沙拉 (yī fèn shālā, one salad).
Salad is a Western import found mainly in Western-style restaurants and cafes; traditional Chinese cuisine has its own cold dishes called 凉拌菜 (liángbàncài, cold tossed dishes) as a rough equivalent.

cards.spanishPhrase: La sopa está muy caliente hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān de tāng hěn rè.
cards.grammarNote:
汤 (tāng) means soup or broth and is a core part of almost every Chinese meal.
Count with 碗: 来一碗汤 (lái yī wǎn tāng, bring a bowl of soup).
Common types: 骨头汤 (gǔtoutāng, bone broth), 蛋花汤 (dànhuātāng, egg-drop soup), 酸辣汤 (suānlàtāng, hot and sour soup).
In Chinese dining, soup is often served throughout the meal or at the end, not just as a Western-style starter.

cards.spanishPhrase: Quiero un sándwich de jamón, por favor.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我想要一个火腿三明治。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiǎng yào yī gè huǒtuǐ sānmíngzhì.
cards.grammarNote:
三明治 (sānmíngzhì) is a phonetic loanword from English 'sandwich' — 三 (sān, three), 明 (míng, bright), 治 (zhì, govern) were chosen for sound, not meaning.
Count with 个: 一个三明治 (yī gè sānmíngzhì, one sandwich).
Sandwiches are a Western-style food found in cafes and convenience stores in China; order with 我要一个三明治 (wǒ yào yī gè sānmíngzhì, I'll have a sandwich).

cards.spanishPhrase: Ella hizo un pastel de chocolate.
cards.chinesePhrase: 她做了一个巧克力蛋糕。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā zuò le yī gè qiǎokèlì dàngāo.
cards.grammarNote:
蛋糕 (dàngāo) lit. 'egg-cake' — 蛋 (dàn) = egg, 糕 (gāo) = cake/pastry; eggs are essential to the batter.
Count with 个 for a whole cake or 块 (kuài) for a slice: 一块蛋糕 (yī kuài dàngāo, a slice of cake).
生日蛋糕 (shēngrì dàngāo) = birthday cake.
Chinese-style pastries often use less sugar and feature savory or bean-based fillings rather than Western-style buttercream.

cards.spanishPhrase: Los niños comen galletas por la tarde.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Háizimen xiàwǔ chī bǐnggān.
cards.grammarNote:
饼干 (bǐnggān) lit. 'biscuit-dry' — 饼 (bǐng) = flat cake/biscuit/pancake, 干 (gān) = dry; together they describe a dry, flat baked snack.
Count with 块 (kuài, piece) or 片 (piàn, flat piece): 一块饼干 (yī kuài bǐnggān, one cookie).
饼干 covers cookies, crackers, and biscuits alike.
The broader 饼 (bǐng) family includes many Chinese flatbreads and pancakes such as 烧饼 (shāobǐng, sesame flatbread).

cards.spanishPhrase: A ella le gusta mucho el chocolate.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā fēicháng xǐhuān qiǎokèlì.
cards.grammarNote:
巧克力 (qiǎokèlì) is a phonetic loanword from English 'chocolate' — 巧 (qiǎo, clever), 克 (kè, gram/overcome), 力 (lì, strength) were chosen for sound, not meaning.
Count pieces with 块 (kuài): 一块巧克力 (yī kuài qiǎokèlì, a piece of chocolate).
Chocolate is popular in modern China but is not a traditional ingredient; it appears widely in desserts and drinks and is a common gift item.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Quieres azúcar en tu café?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ xiǎng zài kāfēi lǐ jiā táng ma?
cards.grammarNote:
糖 (táng) means both sugar and candy/sweets — context distinguishes them.
白糖 (báitáng) = white sugar, 红糖 (hóngtáng) = brown sugar, 冰糖 (bīngtáng) = rock sugar (common in Chinese cooking and herbal remedies).
Measure by spoon: 加一勺糖 (jiā yī sháo táng, add a spoonful of sugar).
糖醋 (tángcù, sweet and sour) is a classic Chinese flavor combination.

cards.spanishPhrase: Esta comida necesita más sal.
cards.chinesePhrase: 这道菜需要多加一点盐。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhè dào cài xūyào duō jiā yīdiǎn yán.
cards.grammarNote:
盐 (yán) is the single character for salt, one of the most fundamental seasonings in Chinese cooking.
Measure by spoon: 加一点盐 (jiā yīdiǎn yán, add a little salt).
The phrase 油盐酱醋 (yóu yán jiàng cù, oil-salt-sauce-vinegar) refers to the four basic kitchen seasonings.
To request less salt at a restaurant: 少放盐 (shǎo fàng yán, put less salt please).

cards.spanishPhrase: Él pone pimienta en todo lo que come.
cards.chinesePhrase: 他在所有菜里都放胡椒。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā zài suǒyǒu cài lǐ dōu fàng hújiāo.
cards.grammarNote:
胡椒 (hújiāo) lit. 'foreign-pepper' — 胡 (hú) is an old term for things from Central Asia or the West, 椒 (jiāo) = pepper/chili.
This specifically means black or white pepper (the spice grind).
Distinguish from 辣椒 (làjiāo, chili pepper) and 花椒 (huājiāo, Sichuan peppercorn).
胡椒粉 (hújiāofěn) is the common ground pepper powder used as a table condiment.

cards.spanishPhrase: Pon mantequilla en el pan, por favor.
cards.chinesePhrase: 请在面包上涂一些黄油。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng zài miànbāo shàng tú yīxiē huángyóu.
cards.grammarNote:
黄油 (huángyóu) lit. 'yellow-oil' — 黄 (huáng) = yellow, 油 (yóu) = oil/fat; the name describes butter's yellow color and fatty nature.
Measure with 块 (kuài, piece/pat): 一块黄油 (yī kuài huángyóu, a pat of butter).
Butter is mainly used in Western-style baking in China; traditional Chinese cooking relies on vegetable oils rather than dairy fats.
涂黄油 (tú huángyóu) = to spread butter.

cards.spanishPhrase: Usamos aceite para freír las verduras.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen yòng yóu lái chǎo shūcài.
cards.grammarNote:
油 (yóu) means oil in general — cooking oil, fat, or even petroleum depending on context.
Common types: 菜籽油 (càizǐyóu, rapeseed oil), 花生油 (huāshēngyóu, peanut oil), 麻油/香油 (máyóu/xiāngyóu, sesame oil).
Measure in 瓶 (píng, bottle): 一瓶油 (yī píng yóu, one bottle of oil).
High-heat oil is essential for 炒 (chǎo, stir-fry), the cornerstone technique of Chinese cooking.

cards.spanishPhrase: Bebo café cada mañana.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ měitiān zǎoshang hē kāfēi.
cards.grammarNote:
咖啡 (kāfēi) is a phonetic loanword — 咖 (kā) and 啡 (fēi) were invented to transcribe this foreign sound and have no independent everyday meaning.
Order with 来一杯咖啡 (lái yī bēi kāfēi, bring a cup of coffee) — 杯 is the measure word.
黑咖啡 (hēi kāfēi) = black coffee, 拿铁 (nǎtiě) = latte, 冰咖啡 (bīng kāfēi) = iced coffee.
China has seen a massive coffee culture boom among younger urban consumers.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Quieres tomar té conmigo?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ xiǎng hé wǒ yīqǐ hē chá ma?
cards.grammarNote:
茶 (chá) is one of the most culturally significant words in Chinese — China is the birthplace of tea culture, and the word itself traveled to most world languages (English 'tea', Spanish 'té').
Order with 来一杯茶 (lái yī bēi chá, bring a cup of tea).
Types: 绿茶 (lǜchá, green tea), 红茶 (hóngchá, black tea — lit. 'red tea'), 乌龙茶 (wūlóngchá, oolong).
Offering tea to guests is a key hospitality ritual in Chinese culture.

cards.spanishPhrase: Bebemos vino en la cena del viernes.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我们星期五的晚饭喝葡萄酒。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen xīngqīwǔ de wǎnfàn hē pútáojiǔ.
cards.grammarNote:
葡萄酒 (pútáojiǔ) lit. 'grape-wine' — 葡萄 (pútáo) = grape, 酒 (jiǔ) = alcoholic drink.
酒 alone covers all alcohol; wine is specified as 'grape alcohol.' Order with 来一杯葡萄酒 (bring a glass of wine) or 来一瓶 (bring a bottle).
Red wine is 红酒 (hóngjiǔ) colloquially; white wine is 白葡萄酒.
Note: standalone 白酒 (báijiǔ) more commonly refers to Chinese grain spirits.

cards.spanishPhrase: Él bebe una cerveza con sus amigos.
cards.chinesePhrase: 他和朋友们一起喝啤酒。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā hé péngyoumen yīqǐ hē píjiǔ.
cards.grammarNote:
啤酒 (píjiǔ) is a phonetic loanword — 啤 (pí) was created to transcribe 'beer', and 酒 (jiǔ) = alcoholic drink.
Order with 来一瓶啤酒 (bring a bottle of beer) or 来一杯 (bring a glass).
Beer is extremely popular in China; brands like 青岛 (Qīngdǎo, Tsingtao) and 雪花 (Xuěhuā, Snow Beer) are among the world's highest-volume beers.
干杯 (gānbēi, cheers — lit. 'dry cup') is the standard toast.

cards.spanishPhrase: Como huevos en el desayuno cada día.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ měitiān zǎocān chī jīdàn.
cards.grammarNote:
早餐 (zǎocān) lit. 'early-meal' — 早 (zǎo) = early/morning, 餐 (cān) = meal.
The more casual word is 早饭 (zǎofàn).
Chinese breakfasts are savory and diverse: 豆浆油条 (dòujiāng yóutiáo, soy milk and fried dough sticks), 包子 (bāozi, steamed buns), 粥 (zhōu, congee/rice porridge).
Ask: 在哪里可以吃早餐？ (Where can I have breakfast?).
Breakfast counters often open as early as 5–6am in China.

cards.spanishPhrase: Comemos juntos el almuerzo a las doce.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我们十二点一起吃午饭。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen shí'èr diǎn yīqǐ chī wǔfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
午饭 (wǔfàn) lit. 'noon-food/rice' — 午 (wǔ) = noon/midday, 饭 (fàn) = cooked rice/meal.
The more formal term is 午餐 (wǔcān).
Chinese lunch is typically a hot, full meal — not a sandwich — often eaten at a canteen (食堂, shítáng) or nearby restaurant.
Lunch hour in China is generally 12–2pm, and many people take a short nap (午睡, wǔshuì) afterward.
Suggest lunch with: 我们去吃午饭吧 (let's go have lunch).

cards.spanishPhrase: Mi familia cena junta cada noche.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我的家人每晚一起吃晚饭。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ de jiārén měi wǎn yīqǐ chī wǎnfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
晚饭 (wǎnfàn) lit. 'evening-food/rice' — 晚 (wǎn) = evening/late, 饭 (fàn) = cooked rice/meal.
The more formal term is 晚餐 (wǎncān).
Dinner is the most social meal in China, with shared dishes placed in the center of the table for everyone to eat from — often on a spinning lazy Susan (转盘, zhuànpán).
Invite someone with 来我家吃晚饭吧 (come to my home for dinner).

cards.spanishPhrase: Los niños comen una merienda después de clase.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Háizimen fàngxué hòu chī língshí.
cards.grammarNote:
零食 (língshí) lit. 'scattered-food' — 零 (líng) = scattered/odd/small, 食 (shí) = food; the compound captures small, informal bites eaten outside of main meals.
Count individual packets with 包 (bāo, bag/packet): 一包零食 (yī bāo língshí, a bag of snacks).
China has a huge snack culture; convenience stores and dedicated snack shops (零食店) carry hundreds of varieties of chips, dried fruits, nuts, and candies.

cards.spanishPhrase: Esta comida es muy deliciosa y nutritiva.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhè yī dùn fàn fēicháng hǎochī.
cards.grammarNote:
一顿饭 (yī dùn fàn) — 顿 (dùn) is the specific measure word for meals: 一顿饭 (one meal), 三顿饭 (three meals a day).
饭 (fàn) = cooked rice/meal.
The phrase 请你吃一顿饭 (qǐng nǐ chī yī dùn fàn, treat you to a meal) expresses gratitude.
Note that 饭 alone can mean 'meal' (吃饭 = eat a meal) or specifically 'cooked rice' depending on context.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Puedes compartir tu receta conmigo?
cards.chinesePhrase: 你能把你的食谱告诉我吗？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ néng bǎ nǐ de shípǔ gàosu wǒ ma?
cards.grammarNote:
食谱 (shípǔ) lit. 'food-guide/score' — 食 (shí) = food, 谱 (pǔ) = score/guide/chart (as in a musical score).
Count recipe books with 本 (běn): 一本食谱 (yī běn shípǔ, a cookbook).
Ask for a recipe: 你能把你的食谱告诉我吗？ (Can you share your recipe with me?).
菜谱 (càipǔ) is a near-synonym, also meaning recipe or restaurant menu.

cards.spanishPhrase: Este helado tiene un sabor muy rico.
cards.chinesePhrase: 这个冰淇淋的味道很好。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhège bīngqílín de wèidào hěn hǎo.
cards.grammarNote:
味道 (wèidào) lit. 'taste-way' — 味 (wèi) = taste/flavor, 道 (dào) = road/way; together they describe the overall sensory impression of food.
Ask: 这个菜味道怎么样？ (How does this dish taste?).
味 alone appears in compounds: 鲜味 (xiānwèi, umami), 甜味 (tiánwèi, sweetness), 苦味 (kǔwèi, bitterness).
味道不错 (wèidào bùcuò, the taste is not bad/pretty good) is a common compliment.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Cuál es el ingrediente más importante?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zuì zhòngyào de shícái shì shénme?
cards.grammarNote:
食材 (shícái) lit. 'food-material' — 食 (shí) = food, 材 (cái) = material.
It refers to raw cooking ingredients.
Count by type with 种 (zhǒng, kind): 这道菜用了三种食材 (this dish uses three ingredients).
In recipes, quantities use 克 (grams), 毫升 (milliliters), or 勺 (spoons).
The near-synonym 原料 (yuánliào, raw materials) is more general; 食材 is specifically for cooking.

cards.spanishPhrase: Por favor, tráigame el menú del restaurante.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng gěi wǒ kàn yīxià càidān.
cards.grammarNote:
菜单 (càidān) lit. 'dish-list' — 菜 (cài) = dish/vegetable, 单 (dān) = list/form.
The key restaurant phrase is 请给我看一下菜单 (please let me see the menu).
点菜 (diǎncài, order dishes) is what you do after reading it.
Look for 今日特价 (jīnrì tèjià, today's special) on any menu.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ella cocina en la cocina cada tarde.
cards.chinesePhrase: 她每天下午在厨房做饭。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā měitiān xiàwǔ zài chúfáng zuòfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
厨房 (chúfáng) lit. 'kitchen-room' — 厨 (chú) = kitchen/cook, 房 (fáng) = room.
The 厨 character also appears in 厨师 (chúshī, chef/cook).
Count rooms with 间 (jiān): 一间厨房 (yī jiān chúfáng, one kitchen).
下厨 (xià chú, go into the kitchen to cook) is an idiomatic expression for cooking at home.

cards.spanishPhrase: Mete el pan en el horno, por favor.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng bǎ miànbāo fàng jìn kǎoxiāng lǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
烤箱 (kǎoxiāng) lit. 'roast-box' — 烤 (kǎo) = to roast/bake/toast, 箱 (xiāng) = box/chest.
Count with the appliance measure word 台 (tái): 一台烤箱 (yī tái kǎoxiāng, one oven).
Ovens are a Western-style appliance less common in traditional Chinese kitchens, which rely on stovetop cooking; they are now widespread in modern homes and bakeries.
预热烤箱 (yùrè kǎoxiāng) = preheat the oven.

cards.spanishPhrase: Calienta la sopa en la estufa.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zài lúzi shàng bǎ tāng rè yīxià.
cards.grammarNote:
炉子 (lúzi) — 炉 (lú) = furnace/stove/hearth, with neutral suffix 子 (zi).
The more specific term for a gas stove is 煤气灶 (méiqìzào) and for an induction cooker 电磁炉 (diàncílú).
Count with 台 (tái): 一台炉子.
Chinese cooking relies heavily on high-heat stovetop stir-frying (爆炒, bàochǎo), so the stove is the heart of the Chinese kitchen.

cards.spanishPhrase: Usa el cuchillo para cortar las verduras.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Yòng càidāo qiē shūcài.
cards.grammarNote:
菜刀 (càidāo) lit. 'vegetable-knife' — 菜 (cài) = vegetable/dish, 刀 (dāo) = knife/blade.
The Chinese chef's knife is a wide rectangular cleaver used for everything from slicing vegetables to chopping bones.
Count with 把 (bǎ, measure word for handled tools): 一把菜刀 (yī bǎ càidāo, one kitchen knife).
刀工 (dāogōng, knife skills) is considered an essential culinary art in Chinese cooking.

cards.spanishPhrase: Necesito un tenedor para comer la ensalada.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我需要一个叉子吃沙拉。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xūyào yī gè chāzi chī shālā.
cards.grammarNote:
叉子 (chāzi) — 叉 (chā) = fork/cross, 子 (zi) is a neutral noun suffix.
Count with 把 (bǎ): 一把叉子 (yī bǎ chāzi, one fork).
Forks are less common than chopsticks (筷子, kuàizi) in Chinese dining but are available in most restaurants serving Western food.
If you need one, ask: 请给我一把叉子 (qǐng gěi wǒ yī bǎ chāzi, please give me a fork).

cards.spanishPhrase: Usa la cuchara para tomar la sopa.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Yòng sháozi hē tāng.
cards.grammarNote:
勺子 (sháozi) — 勺 (sháo) = spoon/ladle, with neutral suffix 子 (zi).
The Chinese soup spoon is typically ceramic and deep.
Count with 把 (bǎ): 一把勺子 (yī bǎ sháozi, one spoon).
一勺 (yī sháo) also works as a measure: 加一勺盐 (jiā yī sháo yán, add a spoonful of salt).
汤勺 (tāngsháo) = soup spoon specifically.

cards.spanishPhrase: Pon la comida en el plato, por favor.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng bǎ shíwù fàng zài pánzi shàng.
cards.grammarNote:
盘子 (pánzi) — 盘 (pán) = plate/tray/disk, with neutral suffix 子 (zi).
Count the object with 个: 一个盘子 (yī gè pánzi, one plate).
Crucially, 盘 is also a measure word for plates of food: 一盘饺子 (yī pán jiǎozi, a plate of dumplings).
In Chinese dining, dishes are placed in the center of the table on 盘子 for everyone to share — individual plated meals per diner are less common in traditional settings.

cards.spanishPhrase: Él come arroz en un tazón grande.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā yòng yī gè dà wǎn chī mǐfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
碗 (wǎn) is both the word for a bowl and a key measure word for bowl-shaped servings.
Count the object with 个: 一个碗 (yī gè wǎn, one bowl).
As a measure word: 一碗米饭 (a bowl of rice), 一碗面 (a bowl of noodles), 一碗汤 (a bowl of soup).
碗 is indispensable for ordering at a Chinese restaurant — almost anything with broth or rice/noodles uses 碗.

cards.spanishPhrase: Hay una botella de agua en la mesa.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhuōzi shàng yǒu yī píng shuǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
瓶子 (píngzi) is the word for a bottle (object) — 瓶 (píng) = bottle, 子 (zi) is a neutral suffix.
瓶 alone is the measure word for bottled things: 一瓶水 (yī píng shuǐ, one bottle of water), 一瓶啤酒 (one bottle of beer).
Count the bottle itself with 个: 一个瓶子 (yī gè píngzi, one bottle).
At a restaurant: 我要一瓶矿泉水 (I'll have a bottle of mineral water).

cards.spanishPhrase: Todos se sientan a la mesa para comer.
cards.chinesePhrase: 大家都坐在桌子旁边吃饭。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Dàjiā dōu zuò zài zhuōzi pángbiān chīfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
桌子 (zhuōzi) — 桌 (zhuō) = table/desk, with neutral suffix 子 (zi).
Count with 张 (zhāng, measure word for flat surfaces like tables and paper): 一张桌子 (yī zhāng zhuōzi, one table).
At a restaurant: 我们需要一张四人桌 (we need a table for four).
Chinese round dining tables often feature a spinning center tray (转盘, zhuànpán) so shared dishes are easy to reach.

cards.spanishPhrase: El camarero nos trae la comida rápido.
cards.chinesePhrase: 服务员很快给我们上菜。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Fúwùyuán hěn kuài gěi wǒmen shàngcài.
cards.grammarNote:
服务员 (fúwùyuán) lit. 'service-person' — 服务 (fúwù) = service, 员 (yuán) = person/staff member.
This term addresses any service worker, not just restaurant waiters — also hotel staff and shop assistants.
To get a waiter's attention in China, simply call out 服务员！ — this is polite and normal.
Follow up with 我们要点菜 (wǒmen yào diǎncài, we'd like to order).

cards.spanishPhrase: Tengo mucha hambre, ¿podemos comer ya?
cards.chinesePhrase: 我很饿，我们可以吃饭了吗？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ hěn è, wǒmen kěyǐ chīfàn le ma?
cards.grammarNote:
饿 (è) means hungry and functions as a state verb — say 我很饿 (wǒ hěn è, I'm very hungry) or 我饿了 (wǒ è le, I've become hungry now).
The 了 (le) version signals a change of state and is very natural in speech.
我饿死了 (wǒ è sǐ le, lit. 'I'm hungry to death') is a common hyperbolic expression meaning 'I'm starving.' The character 饿 contains the 食 (shí, food) radical on the left.

cards.spanishPhrase: Tengo mucha sed después de correr.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Pǎobù zhīhòu wǒ hěn kě.
cards.grammarNote:
渴 (kě) means thirsty and works as a state verb: 我很渴 (wǒ hěn kě, I'm very thirsty) or 我渴了 (wǒ kě le, I've become thirsty).
Like 饿 (hungry), adding 死了 intensifies: 我渴死了 (I'm dying of thirst).
The character 渴 contains the 氵 (water radical) on the left.
Ask for water: 能给我拿一杯水吗？ (Could you bring me a glass of water?).

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Quieres comer algo conmigo ahora?
cards.chinesePhrase: 你现在想和我一起吃东西吗？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ xiànzài xiǎng hé wǒ yīqǐ chī dōngxi ma?
cards.grammarNote:
吃 (chī) is the verb for eating and one of the most-used verbs in Chinese.
The fundamental mealtime expression is 吃饭 (chī fàn, eat rice/eat a meal), used for any meal.
Common patterns: 你吃了吗？ (Have you eaten? — a traditional greeting equivalent to 'how are you?'), 好吃 (hǎochī, delicious — lit. 'good to eat').
吃 pairs freely: 吃面 (eat noodles), 吃肉 (eat meat).

cards.spanishPhrase: Debes beber más agua cada día.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ měitiān yīnggāi duō hē shuǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
喝 (hē) is the verb for drinking any liquid.
Common patterns: 喝水 (hē shuǐ, drink water), 喝茶 (hē chá, drink tea), 喝酒 (hē jiǔ, drink alcohol).
To order: 我想喝... (wǒ xiǎng hē..., I'd like to drink...).
Note that 喝 is also used for soup (喝汤, hē tāng) since Chinese soup is sipped directly from a bowl.
好喝 (hǎohē, tastes good for drinks) parallels 好吃 (hǎochī, tastes good for food).

cards.spanishPhrase: Me gusta cocinar para mi familia.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我喜欢为我的家人做饭。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xǐhuān wèi wǒ de jiārén zuòfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
做饭 (zuòfàn) lit. 'make-food/rice' — the standard phrase for 'to cook' in the sense of preparing a home meal.
A professional cook is 厨师 (chúshī, chef).
Key Chinese cooking techniques: 炒 (chǎo, stir-fry), 蒸 (zhēng, steam), 煮 (zhǔ, boil), 炸 (zhá, deep-fry), 烤 (kǎo, roast/bake).
下厨 (xià chú, go into the kitchen) is another idiomatic expression for cooking.

cards.spanishPhrase: Vamos a freír el pollo para cenar.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen jīn wǎn yào zhá jī.
cards.grammarNote:
炸 (zhá) means to deep-fry in oil — distinct from 炒 (chǎo, stir-fry with less oil).
炸鸡 (zhájī) = fried chicken, 炸薯条 (zhá shǔtiáo) = French fries.
The character 炸 contains the 火 (fire) radical on the left.
Another fry word is 煎 (jiān, pan-fry with a little oil): 煎蛋 (jiāndàn) = fried egg.

cards.spanishPhrase: Asamos carne en el horno los domingos.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我们星期天在烤箱里烤肉。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen xīngqītiān zài kǎoxiāng lǐ kǎo ròu.
cards.grammarNote:
烤 (kǎo) means to roast, bake, grill, or toast — any dry-heat cooking.
Famous examples: 烤鸭 (kǎoyā, Peking roast duck), 烤鸡 (kǎojī, roast chicken), 烤面包 (kǎo miànbāo, toast).
烤 also appears in 烧烤 (shāokǎo, BBQ/grilled food), a hugely popular Chinese street food style.
The character 烤 contains the 火 (fire) radical.

cards.spanishPhrase: Hay que picar bien las cebollas.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xūyào bǎ yángcōng duò suì.
cards.grammarNote:
剁碎 (duò suì) — 剁 (duò) = to chop with a heavy repeated motion (like a cleaver), 碎 (suì) = smashed into small pieces/minced.
Together they mean to chop finely or mince.
This differs from 切 (qiē, to slice) or 切片 (qiēpiàn, to slice into pieces).
剁椒 (duòjiāo, chopped fermented chili) is a famous Hunan condiment.
Use in a sentence: 把肉剁碎 (bǎ ròu duò suì, mince the meat).

cards.spanishPhrase: Por favor, rebana el pan para el desayuno.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng bǎ miànbāo qiēpiàn zuò zǎocān.
cards.grammarNote:
切片 (qiēpiàn) is a verb-object compound: 切 (qiē) = to cut/slice, 片 (piàn) = a flat thin piece.
Together it means 'to slice into pieces.' 片 also serves as a measure word: 一片面包 (yī piàn miànbāo, a slice of bread), 一片肉 (yī piàn ròu, a slice of meat).
Note the difference between 切 (general slicing), 剁 (heavy chopping/mincing), 切丁 (to dice), and 切丝 (to julienne/shred).

cards.spanishPhrase: Revuelve bien la sopa antes de servirla.
cards.chinesePhrase: 上菜前好好搅拌一下汤。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Shàngcài qián hǎohǎo jiǎobàn yīxià tāng.
cards.grammarNote:
搅拌 (jiǎobàn) lit. 'stir-mix' — 搅 (jiǎo) = to stir/disturb, 拌 (bàn) = to mix/toss.
Together they mean to stir or mix ingredients.
Distinguish from 炒 (chǎo, stir-fry over heat) — 搅拌 is mixing without necessarily applying heat.
搅拌机 (jiǎobànjī) = blender/mixer.
Related: 凉拌 (liángbàn, cold-toss) as in 凉拌黄瓜 (cold tossed cucumber, a popular appetizer).

cards.spanishPhrase: Por favor, vierte el agua en el vaso.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng bǎ shuǐ dào jìn bēizi lǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
倒 (dào) means to pour (liquid) or to invert/tip over.
Common uses: 倒水 (dào shuǐ, pour water), 倒茶 (dào chá, pour tea).
The full structure uses 把: 把水倒进杯子里 (pour the water into the glass).
Pouring tea for others before yourself is an important social gesture in Chinese culture — the host always serves guests first.
倒满 (dào mǎn) = pour to full/top up.

cards.spanishPhrase: El camarero viene a servir la cena.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Fúwùyuán lái shàngcài le.
cards.grammarNote:
上菜 (shàngcài) lit. 'bring up dishes' — 上 (shàng) = to go up/bring up/present, 菜 (cài) = dish/food.
This is the specific term for serving food at a restaurant.
Ask: 我们的菜什么时候上？ (When will our food be served?).
上 as 'serve/present' also appears in 上酒 (serve wine) and 上甜点 (bring dessert).

cards.spanishPhrase: Vamos a cenar en ese restaurante esta noche.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我们今晚去那个餐厅用餐。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen jīn wǎn qù nàge cāntīng yòngcān.
cards.grammarNote:
用餐 (yòngcān) lit. 'use/employ a meal' — 用 (yòng) = to use, 餐 (cān) = meal.
This is the formal or polite term for dining, more elevated than 吃饭 (chīfàn, eat a meal).
You'll see it in restaurant signage (用餐区, dining area), hotel menus, and polite speech.
用餐愉快！ (Enjoy your meal!) is the formal way to say 'bon appétit.'

cards.spanishPhrase: Las frutas frescas son muy buenas para ti.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xīnxiān de shuǐguǒ duì nǐ hěn hǎo.
cards.grammarNote:
新鲜 (xīnxiān) lit. 'new-fresh/rare' — 新 (xīn) = new, 鲜 (xiān) = fresh/rare/delicious.
鲜 alone also carries the meaning of umami/savory freshness, appearing in 鲜味 (xiānwèi, umami).
Use as an adjective: 新鲜的鱼 (xīnxiān de yú, fresh fish), 新鲜蔬菜 (fresh vegetables).
Ask at the market: 这个新鲜吗？ (Is this fresh?).
不新鲜 (bù xīnxiān) = not fresh/stale.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ten cuidado, la sopa está muy caliente.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xiǎoxīn, tāng hěn rè.
cards.grammarNote:
热 (rè) describes temperature heat — not spicy heat, which is 辣 (là).
热 is an adjective/state verb: 汤很热 (the soup is very hot), 热茶 (rèchá, hot tea).
The opposite is 冷 (lěng, cold) or 凉 (liáng, cool/lukewarm).
In Chinese culture, hot food and drinks are considered healthier, so you'll often be served 热水 by default.
趁热吃 (chèn rè chī, eat it while it's hot) is a classic mealtime expression.

cards.spanishPhrase: Prefiero el té frío en verano.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xiàtiān wǒ xǐhuān hē lěng chá.
cards.grammarNote:
冷 (lěng) describes cold temperature.
For food and drinks: 冷茶 (lěng chá, cold tea), 冷水 (lěng shuǐ, cold water).
Also useful: 冰 (bīng, ice/iced) as a prefix — 冰水 (bīngshuǐ, iced water), 冰咖啡 (bīng kāfēi, iced coffee).
Traditionally, cold drinks are thought to harm digestion in Chinese culture, so ordering cold water may prompt a surprised reaction.
凉 (liáng) means cool or room-temperature, slightly different from fully cold 冷.

cards.spanishPhrase: Este pastel está muy dulce y rico.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhège dàngāo hěn tián hěn hǎochī.
cards.grammarNote:
甜 (tián) is the adjective for sweet taste: 这个很甜 (zhège hěn tián, this is very sweet).
甜点 (tiándiǎn, dessert — lit. 'sweet dot/snack') is the general word for dessert.
Chinese desserts are typically less sweet than Western ones; traditional sweets include 汤圆 (tāngyuán, sweet glutinous rice balls) and 红豆汤 (hóngdòutāng, sweet red bean soup).

cards.spanishPhrase: El limón tiene un sabor muy agrio.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Níngméng de wèidào hěn suān.
cards.grammarNote:
酸 (suān) means sour/tart and is one of the five classic Chinese flavor categories (酸甜苦辣咸: sour-sweet-bitter-spicy-salty).
酸辣汤 (suānlàtāng) = hot and sour soup; 糖醋 (tángcù) = sweet and sour; 酸奶 (suānnǎi) = yogurt (lit. 'sour milk').
酸 can also mean 'aching/sore' for muscles, so context matters.
Exclaim: 太酸了！ (tài suān le!, way too sour!).

cards.spanishPhrase: Este café sin azúcar está muy amargo.
cards.chinesePhrase: 这杯不加糖的咖啡很苦。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhè bēi bù jiā táng de kāfēi hěn kǔ.
cards.grammarNote:
苦 (kǔ) means bitter in taste and is also widely used to mean 'hard/painful/suffering' — 吃苦 (chī kǔ, to endure hardship — lit. 'eat bitter').
In food: 苦咖啡 (kǔ kāfēi, bitter coffee), 苦瓜 (kǔguā, bitter melon).
苦 is one of the five flavor categories.
Bitter melon is eaten for its perceived health benefits and is an acquired taste even for many Chinese people.

cards.spanishPhrase: Esta salsa está muy picante para mí.
cards.chinesePhrase: 这个酱对我来说太辣了。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhège jiàng duì wǒ lái shuō tài là le.
cards.grammarNote:
辣 (là) means spicy/hot from chili peppers — distinct from 热 (rè, hot in temperature).
辣 is a cornerstone flavor of Sichuan and Hunan cuisines.
Common expressions: 太辣了 (too spicy), 不辣 (not spicy), 微辣 (wēilà, mildly spicy).
At a restaurant: 不要太辣 (please don't make it too spicy).
麻辣 (málà, numbing-spicy) is the Sichuan combination of Sichuan peppercorn and chili.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¡Esta pizza está absolutamente deliciosa!
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhège pīsà zhēn de hěn hǎochī!
cards.grammarNote:
好吃 (hǎochī) lit. 'good to eat' — 好 (hǎo) = good, 吃 (chī) = eat; the pattern 好 + verb means 'good to [verb].' The equivalent for drinks is 好喝 (hǎohē, good to drink).
Compliment food with 真的很好吃！(zhēn de hěn hǎochī!, it's really delicious!).
A stronger expression is 太好吃了！(tài hǎochī le!, SO delicious!) or 美味 (měiwèi, delicious — lit. 'beautiful flavor').

cards.spanishPhrase: Esta sopa está un poco sosa hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān zhè wǎn tāng yǒudiǎn dàn.
cards.grammarNote:
淡 (dàn) means bland, mild, or light in flavor — specifically lacking enough salt or seasoning.
这道菜太淡了 (zhè dào cài tài dàn le, this dish is too bland).
淡 also means light in color or diluted: 淡茶 (dàn chá, weak/light tea).
The opposite is 咸 (xián, salty).
To request more seasoning: 能加点盐吗？ (Can you add a little salt?).
淡淡的 (dàndàn de) can also describe a delicate, subtle flavor positively.

cards.spanishPhrase: Las verduras son una comida muy saludable.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Shūcài shì hěn jiànkāng de shíwù.
cards.grammarNote:
健康的 (jiànkāng de) lit. 'healthy' — 健 (jiàn) = strong/healthy, 康 (kāng) = well-being/peace.
The 的 (de) marks it as an adjective modifying a noun: 健康的食物 (healthy food).
Chinese food culture has deep roots in the idea that food is medicine (药食同源, yào shí tóngyuán), so 健康 is a very common concept when discussing eating.
健康 alone functions as the noun 'health': 为了健康 (for the sake of health).

cards.spanishPhrase: Comer demasiada azúcar es muy insalubre.
cards.chinesePhrase: 吃太多糖对身体不健康。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Chī tài duō táng duì shēntǐ bù jiànkāng.
cards.grammarNote:
不健康的 (bù jiànkāng de) is the negation of 健康的 — 不 (bù) = not, plus 健康的 (healthy).
The structure 不 + adjective is the standard negation pattern: 不辣 (not spicy), 不甜 (not sweet).
In speech, 这个不健康 (this is unhealthy) without 的 is also common.
垃圾食品 (lājī shípǐn, junk food — lit. 'trash food') is the colloquial term for unhealthy processed snack foods.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ella es vegetariana y no come carne.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā shì sùshí de, bù chī ròu.
cards.grammarNote:
素食的 (sùshí de) lit. 'plain-food' — 素 (sù) = plain/vegetarian/unadorned, 食 (shí) = food, 的 makes it adjectival.
素 alone is widely understood: 我吃素 (wǒ chī sù, I'm vegetarian).
At a restaurant: 有没有素菜？ (Do you have vegetarian dishes?).
Chinese Buddhist vegetarian cuisine (斋菜, zhāicài) is a rich culinary tradition.
荤 (hūn) is the opposite — meat-based/non-vegetarian food.

cards.spanishPhrase: Él es vegano y no come nada animal.
cards.chinesePhrase: 他是纯素的，不吃任何动物食品。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā shì chúnsù de, bù chī rènhé dòngwù shípǐn.
cards.grammarNote:
纯素的 (chúnsù de) lit. 'pure-vegetarian' — 纯 (chún) = pure/complete, 素 (sù) = vegetarian/plant-based.
纯素 distinguishes from regular vegetarianism (素食) by excluding all animal products including dairy and eggs.
Veganism is a newer concept in China; you might explain: 我不吃肉，也不吃奶蛋 (I don't eat meat, dairy, or eggs).
Buddhist strict vegetarians (全素, quánsù) similarly avoid all animal products.

cards.spanishPhrase: Hay comida congelada en el refrigerador.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Bīngxiāng lǐ yǒu lěngdòng de shíwù.
cards.grammarNote:
冷冻的 (lěngdòng de) lit. 'cold-frozen' — 冷 (lěng) = cold, 冻 (dòng) = to freeze/frozen, 的 makes it adjectival.
冷冻食品 (lěngdòng shípǐn) = frozen food.
冰箱 (bīngxiāng, refrigerator — lit. 'ice box') is where frozen food is stored.
Frozen dumplings (冷冻饺子) are popular convenience items.
解冻 (jiědòng, to defrost/thaw — lit. 'untie the freeze') is what you do before cooking.

cards.spanishPhrase: Compramos verduras orgánicas en el mercado.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我们在市场买有机的蔬菜。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen zài shìchǎng mǎi yǒujī de shūcài.
cards.grammarNote:
有机的 (yǒujī de) lit. 'having-organic-structure' — 有 (yǒu) = to have, 机 (jī) = mechanism/organic chemistry structure, 的 makes it adjectival.
有机食品 (yǒujī shípǐn) = organic food.
Organic produce is increasingly popular in Chinese cities, sold at specialty markets and premium supermarkets.
The label 认证有机 (rènzhèng yǒujī, certified organic) signals government-approved certification.

cards.spanishPhrase: Esta sopa tiene demasiada sal.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhè wǎn tāng tài xián le.
cards.grammarNote:
太 (tài) means 'too/excessively' and is almost always paired with 了 (le) at the end of the phrase: 太辣了 (tài là le, too spicy), 太甜了 (too sweet), 太贵了 (too expensive).
The structure is: 太 + adjective + 了.
Without 了, the sentence feels incomplete in speech.
Interestingly, 太...了 can also express positive extremes: 太好吃了！(SO delicious!) — tone and context determine whether the meaning is negative or enthusiastically positive.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me gusta estar en casa los fines de semana.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xǐhuān zhōumò dāi zài jiā lǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
家 (jiā) is both 'home' and 'family' — a beautifully compact word.
Use 在家 (zài jiā) = 'at home' and 回家 (huí jiā) = 'go home / return home.' 家 also functions as a measure word for businesses: 一家餐厅 (yī jiā cāntīng) = one restaurant.
No article is used in Chinese — 家 stands alone with no equivalent of 'the' or 'a.'

cards.spanishPhrase: Ella vive en un apartamento pequeño.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā zhù zài yī gè xiǎo gōngyù lǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
公寓 (gōngyù) lit. 'public-lodging' — the standard word for apartment or flat.
The measure word is 一套公寓 (yī tào gōngyù), using 套 (tào), which implies a complete unit or suite.
住在公寓里 (zhù zài gōngyù lǐ) = live in an apartment; 里 adds the sense of 'inside.' Compare 宿舍 (sùshè) = dormitory.

cards.spanishPhrase: Los niños van a la escuela cada mañana.
cards.chinesePhrase: 孩子们每天早上去学校。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Háizimen měitiān zǎoshang qù xuéxiào.
cards.grammarNote:
学校 (xuéxiào) lit. 'study-school' — the general word for any school.
上学 (shàng xué) = to attend school (lit. 'ascend study'), used for children going to class.
Use 去学校 (qù xuéxiào) for physically going to the school building.
Distinguish 小学 (xiǎoxué) = primary school, 中学 (zhōngxué) = middle/high school, and 大学 (dàxué) = university.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ella estudia medicina en la universidad.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā zài dàxué xuéxí yīxué.
cards.grammarNote:
大学 (dàxué) lit. 'big study' — university or college.
上大学 (shàng dàxué) = to attend university; 考大学 (kǎo dàxué) = to sit the university entrance exam.
Use 在大学 (zài dàxué) for 'at university.' The measure word for universities is 所 (suǒ): 一所大学 (yī suǒ dàxué).

cards.spanishPhrase: Él trabaja en la oficina todos los días.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā měitiān zài bàngōngshì gōngzuò.
cards.grammarNote:
办公室 (bàngōngshì) lit. 'handle-work-room' — an office or workspace.
在办公室 (zài bàngōngshì) = at the office; 去办公室 (qù bàngōngshì) = go to the office.
The measure word is 间 (jiān) for a single room: 一间办公室.
Contrast with 公司 (gōngsī) = company, which refers to the whole business.

cards.spanishPhrase: Mi madre trabaja en el hospital de aquí.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我妈妈在这里的医院工作。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ māma zài zhèlǐ de yīyuàn gōngzuò.
cards.grammarNote:
医院 (yīyuàn) lit. 'medicine-courtyard' — the standard word for hospital.
去医院 (qù yīyuàn) = go to the hospital; no article needed.
住院 (zhùyuàn) = to be hospitalized; 出院 (chūyuàn) = to be discharged.
Compare 诊所 (zhěnsuǒ) = clinic, a smaller medical facility.

cards.spanishPhrase: Voy a la tienda de abarrotes a comprar fruta.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ qù chāoshì mǎi shuǐguǒ.
cards.grammarNote:
超市 (chāoshì) lit. 'super-market' — the everyday word for supermarket or grocery store.
去超市 (qù chāoshì) = go to the supermarket.
The measure word for stores is 家 (jiā): 一家超市.
A more formal word for market is 市场 (shìchǎng), while 便利店 (biànlìdiàn) = convenience store.

cards.spanishPhrase: Necesito ir al banco esta tarde.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我今天下午需要去银行。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīntiān xiàwǔ xūyào qù yínháng.
cards.grammarNote:
银行 (yínháng) lit. 'silver-firm/row' — 银 means silver (historically, currency) and 行 means a business firm or row.
去银行 (qù yínháng) = go to the bank.
Common phrases: 取钱 (qǔ qián) = withdraw money, 存钱 (cún qián) = deposit money.
Measure word: 一家银行 (yī jiā yínháng).

cards.spanishPhrase: Ella lee libros en la biblioteca todos los días.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā měitiān zài túshūguǎn kàn shū.
cards.grammarNote:
图书馆 (túshūguǎn) lit. 'picture-book-hall' — 图书 = books/publications, 馆 = a public hall or venue.
在图书馆看书 (zài túshūguǎn kàn shū) = read at the library.
The measure word is 座 (zuò) for large buildings: 一座图书馆.
Compare 书店 (shūdiàn) = bookstore, where you buy rather than borrow.

cards.spanishPhrase: Vamos a cenar en ese restaurante esta noche.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我们今晚去那家餐厅吃饭。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen jīn wǎn qù nà jiā cāntīng chīfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
餐厅 (cāntīng) lit. 'meal-hall' — a common, neutral word for restaurant.
The essential measure word is 家 (jiā): 一家餐厅 (yī jiā cāntīng).
去餐厅吃饭 (qù cāntīng chīfàn) = go to a restaurant to eat.
A more casual alternative is 饭馆 (fànguǎn) or 饭店 (fàndiàn), though 饭店 can also mean hotel.

cards.spanishPhrase: Nos encontramos en el café a las diez.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我们十点在咖啡厅见面。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen shí diǎn zài kāfēitīng jiànmiàn.
cards.grammarNote:
咖啡厅 (kāfēitīng) lit. 'coffee-hall' — a café or coffee shop.
Like restaurants, use the measure word 家 (jiā): 一家咖啡厅.
在咖啡厅见面 (zài kāfēitīng jiànmiàn) = meet at a café.
A shorter, more casual form is 咖啡店 (kāfēidiàn).
Note that 咖啡 (kāfēi) is a phonetic borrowing from the English/Portuguese word 'coffee.'

cards.spanishPhrase: Nos quedamos en un hotel cerca del mar.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我们住在海边的一家酒店。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen zhù zài hǎibiān de yī jiā jiǔdiàn.
cards.grammarNote:
酒店 (jiǔdiàn) lit. 'wine-shop/inn' — the modern standard word for hotel.
The measure word is 家 (jiā): 一家酒店.
住酒店 (zhù jiǔdiàn) = to stay at a hotel.
Note that 饭店 (fàndiàn) also commonly means hotel in mainland Chinese usage, though it literally means 'rice/food shop.' 旅馆 (lǚguǎn) is an older term for a simpler inn or guesthouse.

cards.spanishPhrase: Tenemos que llegar al aeropuerto a tiempo.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我们需要准时到达机场。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen xūyào zhǔnshí dàodá jīchǎng.
cards.grammarNote:
机场 (jīchǎng) lit. 'machine-field/ground' — 机 here refers to airplane (飞机, fēijī), and 场 = a large open ground or venue.
去机场 (qù jīchǎng) = go to the airport; 到达机场 (dàodá jīchǎng) = arrive at the airport.
Related travel words: 登机口 (dēngjīkǒu) = gate, 值机 (zhíjī) = check-in for a flight, 航班 (hángbān) = flight.

cards.spanishPhrase: Te espero en la estación de tren.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zài huǒchē chēzhàn děng nǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
车站 (chēzhàn) lit. 'vehicle-stop/station' — a general word for any land transport terminal.
Specify the type: 火车站 (huǒchēzhàn) = train station (lit. 'fire-car-station'), 地铁站 (dìtiězhàn) = metro station.
在车站等 (zài chēzhàn děng) = wait at the station.
站 alone can also mean 'stop,' as in a single bus or subway stop.

cards.spanishPhrase: Espera el autobús en la parada de autobús.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zài gōngjiāo zhàn děng gōngjiāo chē.
cards.grammarNote:
公交站 (gōngjiāo zhàn) lit. 'public-transport-stop' — the everyday bus stop.
公交车 (gōngjiāochē) is the standard term for city bus.
等公交车 (děng gōngjiāochē) = wait for the bus; 坐公交车 (zuò gōngjiāochē) = take the bus (坐 = sit/ride).
Also common: 公共汽车 (gōnggòng qìchē) = public bus, a slightly more formal equivalent.

cards.spanishPhrase: Paseamos en el parque por las tardes.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen xiàwǔ zài gōngyuán sànbù.
cards.grammarNote:
公园 (gōngyuán) lit. 'public-garden/park' — 公 = public, 园 = enclosed garden.
Measure word: 一个公园 (yī gè gōngyuán).
在公园散步 (zài gōngyuán sànbù) = take a walk in the park.
Chinese has no article, so 去公园 simply means 'go to the park.' 园 appears in many compound words: 动物园 (dòngwùyuán) = zoo, 植物园 (zhíwùyuán) = botanical garden.

cards.spanishPhrase: Hay mucha gente en la plaza hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān guǎngchǎng shàng yǒu hěn duō rén.
cards.grammarNote:
广场 (guǎngchǎng) lit. 'wide-open ground' — 广 = broad/wide, 场 = open area or venue.
Refers to a large public square or plaza.
在广场上 (zài guǎngchǎng shàng) = on the square; 上 emphasizes the surface of an open space.
The most famous example is 天安门广场 (Tiān'ānmén Guǎngchǎng).
Measure word: 一个广场.

cards.spanishPhrase: Hay muchas tiendas en esta calle.
cards.chinesePhrase: 这条街道上有很多商店。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhè tiáo jiēdào shàng yǒu hěn duō shāngdiàn.
cards.grammarNote:
街道 (jiēdào) lit. 'street-road' — both characters suggest a road or thoroughfare, making the compound emphatic.
The measure word for streets is 条 (tiáo, for long, narrow things): 一条街道.
In casual speech, 街 (jiē) alone is common: 这条街 = this street.
在街上 (zài jiē shàng) = on the street.
Compare 路 (lù) = road, used in many street names.

cards.spanishPhrase: Cruzamos el río por ese puente viejo.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我们走过那座老桥过河。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen zǒuguò nà zuò lǎo qiáo guò hé.
cards.grammarNote:
桥 (qiáo) is the standard word for bridge.
The measure word is 座 (zuò), used for large fixed structures: 一座桥 (yī zuò qiáo).
过桥 (guò qiáo) = cross a bridge; 走过那座桥 = walk across that bridge.
桥 appears in place names across China, e.g.
石桥 (shíqiáo) = stone bridge.
No article equivalent in Chinese — context makes it clear.

cards.spanishPhrase: El banco está en la esquina de la calle.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Yínháng jiù zài jiējiǎo.
cards.grammarNote:
街角 (jiējiǎo) lit. 'street-corner' — the corner where two streets meet.
在街角 (zài jiējiǎo) = at the corner.
A common direction phrase: 在街角转弯 (zài jiējiǎo zhuǎnwān) = turn at the corner.
An alternative expression is 路口 (lùkǒu), lit. 'road-mouth,' which often refers more broadly to an intersection or junction.

cards.spanishPhrase: El museo está en el centro de la ciudad.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Bówùguǎn zài shì zhōngxīn.
cards.grammarNote:
市中心 (shì zhōngxīn) lit. 'city-center' — 市 = city/municipality, 中心 = center.
在市中心 (zài shì zhōngxīn) = in the city center/downtown.
去市中心 (qù shì zhōngxīn) = go downtown.
A synonym is 市区 (shìqū) = urban district.
