cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā shuōhuà zhīqián shēnshēn de tànle kǒu qì.
cards.grammarNote:
叹气 (tànqì) = to sigh.
叹 = to sigh/lament, 气 = breath/air/energy.
Literal: to exhale breath in lament → to sigh.
叹 contains the 口 (mouth) radical — the sound comes from the mouth.
深深地叹了口气 = 'deeply sighed a breath.' The measure 口气 (kǒu qì) = 'a breath' is the natural object of 叹: 叹了一口气 = sighed once.
深深地 = deeply (adverb: adjective reduplicated + 地).
Reduplication of adjectives intensifies: 深→深深, 慢→慢慢.

cards.spanishPhrase: Él frunció el ceño al leer la nota.
cards.chinesePhrase: 他读那张便条时皱了眉。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā dú nà zhāng biàntiáo shí zhòu le méi.
cards.grammarNote:
皱眉 (zhòuméi) = to frown / knit one's brows.
皱 = to wrinkle/crease, 眉 = eyebrow.
The fuller form in the phrase: 皱起了眉头 = 'wrinkled up the brow.' 眉头 (méitóu) = the brow/forehead area between the eyebrows.
起 as a directional complement suggests an upward or emerging motion: 皱起 = wrinkle up.
对 + question/topic = 'regarding / in response to': 对这个问题皱眉 = frowned at this question.

cards.spanishPhrase: Necesito relajarme después del trabajo.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我下班后需要放松一下。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiàbān hòu xūyào fàngsōng yīxià.
cards.grammarNote:
放松 (fàngsōng) = to relax / loosen up.
放 = to release/let go, 松 = loose/slack.
Literal: to let go and loosen → to relax.
A very natural image of tension releasing.
一下 (yīxià) after a verb softens it and suggests doing the action briefly or just a little: 放松一下 = relax a bit.
试着 (shìzhe) = 'try to' (in the sense of making an attempt): 试着放松 = try to relax.
Compare 试试 (shìshi) = give it a try (verb reduplication).
Related: 放松心情 = relax one's mood, 轻松 (qīngsōng) = light and relaxed (adjective).
Adverb form: 生气地 / 愤怒地 (shēngqì de / fènnù de - angrily). Add 地 after the adjective. E.g., '他生气地走了。' (He left angrily.)
cards.spanishPhrase: Él estaba muy enojado ayer.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā zuótiān hěn shēngqì.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ella se puso muy enfadada de repente.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā tūrán biàn de hěn fānù.
cards.grammarNote:
发怒 (fānù) = to fly into a rage / lose one's temper.
发 = to emit/break out (as in 发火 = to flare up), 怒 = fury/rage (contains 心 heart at bottom).
因...而... (yīn...ér...) = because of...therefore... (formal pattern): 因压力而发怒 = got angry because of stress.
Compare angry words: 生气 (shēngqì) = everyday annoyed/angry, 发怒 (fānù) = erupt in rage, 愤怒 (fènnù) = deep anger/fury (noun or state), 发火 (fāhuǒ) = blow one's top (colloquial).
Adverb form: 焦虑地 (jiāolǜ de - anxiously). Add 地 after the adjective. E.g., '她焦虑地等待着。' (She waited anxiously.)
cards.spanishPhrase: Me siento ansioso antes del examen.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Kǎoshì qián wǒ gǎndào hěn jiāolǜ.

cards.grammarNote: Adverb form: 紧张地 (jǐnzhāng de - nervously). Add 地 after the adjective. E.g., '他紧张地说话。' (He spoke nervously.)
cards.spanishPhrase: Estaba nervioso antes de la presentación.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Yǎnjiǎng qián wǒ hěn jǐnzhāng.

cards.grammarNote: Adverb form: 兴奋地 (xīngfèn de - excitedly). Add 地 after the adjective. E.g., '她兴奋地叫了起来。' (She cried out excitedly.)
cards.spanishPhrase: Los niños están muy emocionados hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Háizimen jīntiān hěn xīngfèn.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me siento muy estresado últimamente.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zuìjìn hěn yǒu yālì.
cards.grammarNote:
有压力 (yǒu yālì) = to have/feel stress (literally 'have pressure').
有 + noun describes states very naturally in Chinese.
压力 (yālì) = pressure/stress: 压 = to press down, 力 = strength/force.
The phrase structure: 感到 + 压力 + 很大 = 'feel that the pressure is very big' → feel very stressed.
Note: 大 and 小 are used for intensity of abstract nouns: 压力大 (a lot of stress), 压力小 (little stress).
Chinese often avoids 'much/little' in favor of 'big/small' for abstract quantities.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me siento abrumado con tanto trabajo.
cards.chinesePhrase: 这么多工作让我不堪重负。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhème duō gōngzuò ràng wǒ bùkān zhòngfù.
cards.grammarNote:
不堪重负 (bùkān zhòngfù) = overwhelmed / unable to bear a heavy burden.
A four-character set expression (成语-like).
不堪 = unable to bear / can't stand, 重负 = heavy burden (重 = heavy, 负 = burden/load).
被 (bèi) passive construction: 她被工作量压得不堪重负 = 'She was pressed by the workload to the point of being overwhelmed.' Structure: Subject + 被 + agent + verb + 得 + degree/result.
The 得 here introduces the result of the action.
工作量 (gōngzuòliàng) = workload (量 = quantity/amount).

cards.spanishPhrase: Ella es muy energética por la mañana.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā zǎoshang jīnglì chōngpèi.
cards.grammarNote:
精力充沛 (jīnglì chōngpèi) = full of energy / energetic.
A very common set phrase.
精力 = vitality/energy, 充沛 = overflowing/abundant (充 = fill up, 沛 = abundant/flourishing).
Used as a predicate: 他精力充沛 (He is full of energy).
Also as a modifier: 精力充沛的人 (an energetic person).
醒来 (xǐng lái) = to wake up (and come to).
来 as a directional complement suggests coming into a state — returning to consciousness.
感到 + adjective: 感到精力充沛 = felt energetic.
Adverb form: 疲惫地 (píbèi de - wearily). Add 地 after the adjective. E.g., '他疲惫地拖着脚步。' (He dragged his feet wearily.)
cards.spanishPhrase: Estoy muy cansado después del trabajo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Gōngzuò hòu wǒ hěn lèi.

cards.spanishPhrase: Estoy agotado después de correr.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Pǎobù zhīhòu wǒ jīnpí lìjìn.
cards.grammarNote:
筋疲力尽 (jīnpí lìjìn) = completely exhausted.
A four-character set expression (成语).
筋疲 = muscles exhausted (筋 = tendons/muscles, 疲 = tired), 力尽 = strength all used up (力 = strength, 尽 = exhausted/used up).
Every part of this phrase emphasizes total depletion — muscles tired, strength gone.
Compare: 累 (lèi) = tired (everyday), 疲惫 (píbèi) = exhausted (more formal), 筋疲力尽 = utterly drained (vivid set phrase).
比赛结束后 = after the race finished — 结束 (jiéshù) = to end/finish.

cards.spanishPhrase: El niño es muy curioso sobre los animales.
cards.chinesePhrase: 那个孩子对动物很好奇。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nà gè háizi duì dòngwù hěn hàoqí.
cards.grammarNote:
好奇 (hàoqí) = curious.
好 (hào, 4th tone here) = to like/be fond of, 奇 = strange/unusual.
Note the tone: 好 in 好奇 is 4th tone (hào = to be fond of), NOT 3rd tone (hǎo = good).
好奇 = 'fond of the strange' → curious.
Other 好-as-a-verb compounds: 好学 (hàoxué) = eager to learn, 好胜 (hàoshèng) = competitive.
对...很好奇 = 'very curious about...': 对一切都很好奇 = curious about everything.
一切 (yīqiè) = everything, always paired with 都 (all).

cards.spanishPhrase: Me duele la cabeza hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīntiān tóuténg.
cards.grammarNote:
头 (tóu) = head.
Also used as a suffix to form nouns: 石头 (shítou, stone), 骨头 (gǔtou, bone).
头疼 (tóuténg) = headache — body part + 疼 is the standard Chinese pattern for aches: 肚子疼 (stomachache), 腿疼 (leg pain).
In 我头疼, no possession marker 的 is needed — in Chinese, body parts used as topics are naturally understood as belonging to the subject.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ella tiene el pelo muy largo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā de tóufa hěn cháng.
cards.grammarNote:
头发 (tóufa) = head hair, where 头 (head) + 发 (grow/hair).
发 alone means 'to grow' or 'to send out' — 头发 specifies the hair that grows from the head.
一头 is a special measure word meaning 'a head of (hair)' — used only for hair: 一头长发 (a head of long hair).
For animals, 一头 means 'one head of cattle/pig.' Note: 发 in 头发 is often pronounced with a neutral tone (tóufa), not the 4th tone (fà) it carries alone.

cards.spanishPhrase: Él tiene una cara muy amable.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā de liǎn kàn qǐlái hěn yǒuhǎo.
cards.grammarNote:
脸 (liǎn) = face.
Related: 脸色 (liǎnsè) = complexion/face color, 脸颊 (liǎnjiá) = cheek.
洗脸 is a verb-object compound meaning 'wash (one's) face'.
Chinese often omits the possessive pronoun for routine actions on one's own body: 洗脸 (wash face), 刷牙 (brush teeth), 梳头 (comb hair).
Note: 脸 has the 月 (flesh) radical on the left — many body-related characters share this radical.

cards.spanishPhrase: Tiene los ojos muy bonitos.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā de yǎnjing hěn piàoliang.
cards.grammarNote:
眼睛 (yǎnjing) = eyes.
眼 (yǎn) = eye; 睛 (jīng) = pupil/eyeball.
Both characters contain the 目 (eye) radical.
Alone, 眼 can refer to the eye, but 眼睛 is the full everyday word.
一双 (yī shuāng) is the measure word for paired things: 一双眼睛 (a pair of eyes), 一双手 (a pair of hands), 一双鞋 (a pair of shoes).
睛 is only used in eye-related words, so whenever you see 睛 you know it relates to eyes.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me duele la oreja derecha.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ de yòu ěrduo téng.
cards.grammarNote:
耳朵 (ěrduo) = ear.
耳 is the character for ear and is itself a pictograph of an ear shape.
朵 is a suffix (originally a measure word for flowers) added to make it sound more natural.
受伤 (shòushāng) = to get injured / be hurt — a compound verb: 受 (receive) + 伤 (wound).
The 了 at the end signals a change of state (he is now injured).
Paired body parts like ears: for 'both ears' say 两只耳朵 — 只 (zhī) is the measure word for paired body parts.

cards.spanishPhrase: Tengo la nariz tapada hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīntiān bízi bù tōngqì.
cards.grammarNote:
鼻子 (bízi) = nose.
The character 鼻 is a pictograph — the upper part shows a self-referential 'self' radical (自, which also means 'self' and was originally a drawing of a nose) with the lower element 畀 indicating sound.
Chinese people historically pointed to their nose to indicate 'me,' hence 自 (self) and 鼻 (nose) share history.
子 (zi) is a diminutive/nominalizing suffix on many body part words: 鼻子 (nose), 脖子 (neck), 肚子 (belly), 耳朵 (ear uses 朵 similarly).
堵了 = blocked/stuffed up, 了 shows it's a new state.

cards.spanishPhrase: Abre la boca y di aah.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhāngkāi zuǐba shuō a.
cards.grammarNote:
嘴巴 (zuǐba) = mouth.
嘴 alone is also used (especially in compounds like 嘴唇 lips).
巴 is a suffix making it sound more colloquial — similar to 子.
张开 (zhāngkāi) = open (wide).
张 specifically means to spread/open outward, used mainly for mouth and similar openings.
Compare 开门 (open a door, using 开 alone) vs.
张开嘴 (open your mouth).
嘴 contains the 口 (mouth) radical, which appears in many speech/mouth-related characters.

cards.spanishPhrase: Tiene los labios muy rojos.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā de zuǐchún hěn hóng.
cards.grammarNote:
嘴唇 (zuǐchún) = lips.
嘴 (mouth) + 唇 (lip).
唇 has the 口 (mouth) radical at the bottom.
In Chinese, adjectives describing a state don't need a linking verb like 'is' — instead, 很 (very) serves as a slight intensifier and connector: 嘴唇很干 = 'lips are dry'.
Dropping 很 would sound like a comparison ('her lips are dry, (unlike someone else's)').
Body parts in Chinese are naturally plural in meaning: 嘴唇 refers to both lips.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me duele un diente.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ de yì kē yáchǐ hěn tòng.
cards.grammarNote:
牙齿 (yáchǐ) = teeth (formal/general).
In everyday speech, just 牙 (yá) is common: 刷牙 (brush teeth), 牙疼 (toothache), 牙医 (dentist).
刷牙 is a verb-object compound: 刷 (scrub/brush) + 牙 (tooth).
The compound is used as a unit — to add a duration, the verb is repeated: 刷牙刷两分钟 (brush teeth for two minutes).
The 牙 character contains the tooth shape in its structure — it is a pictographic character.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me mordí la lengua.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ yǎo dào le wǒ de shétou.
cards.grammarNote:
舌头 (shétou) = tongue.
舌 (shé) is the character for tongue, originally a pictograph.
头 here is a nominalizing suffix (same as in 石头 stone, 骨头 bone) — it lightens the tone.
烫 (tàng) = to scald / burn with heat.
Different from 烧 (shāo, to burn with fire) — 烫 specifically means burning from hot liquids or surfaces.
了 after 烫 indicates completion of the action: the food scalded the tongue (and it happened).

cards.spanishPhrase: Me duele el cuello hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīntiān bózi hěn tòng.
cards.grammarNote:
脖子 (bózi) = neck.
脖 has the 月 (flesh) radical on the left — shared with many body-part characters: 脸 (face), 胸 (chest), 腿 (leg), 背 (back), 腰 (waist).
子 (zi) is the diminutive/nominalizing suffix: 脖子 (neck), 鼻子 (nose), 肚子 (belly).
僵 (jiāng) = stiff / rigid.
Used for stiff muscles or joints: 脖子僵 (stiff neck), 手僵了 (hands have gone stiff, e.g., from cold).

cards.spanishPhrase: Levanta el brazo derecho.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng jǔ qǐ nǐ de yòu shǒubì.
cards.grammarNote:
手臂 (shǒubì) = arm.
手 (hand) + 臂 (upper arm).
臂 contains the 月 (flesh) radical.
摔断了 is a verb + resultative complement structure: 摔 (fall/drop) + 断 (break/snap) + 了 (completed).
This pattern describes an action and its result: 摔断 (fall and break), 撞伤 (bump and injure), 切破 (cut and pierce).
For paired body parts: 一只手臂 (one arm) — 只 (zhī) is the measure word for individual items of a pair.

cards.spanishPhrase: Lávate las manos antes de comer.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Chīfàn qián qǐng xǐ shǒu.
cards.grammarNote:
手 (shǒu) = hand.
A pictographic character — the original form depicted five fingers.
Very productive: 手指 (finger), 手臂 (arm), 手腕 (wrist), 手套 (gloves), 手机 (mobile phone — 'hand machine').
Chinese doesn't mark singular/plural — 手 can mean 'a hand' or 'hands' depending on context.
洗手 = wash (your) hands — both understood from context.
For measure words: 一只手 (one hand, using 只 zhī for single paired body parts), 一双手 (a pair of hands).

cards.spanishPhrase: Me corté el dedo con un cuchillo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ yòng dāo qiē dào le shǒuzhǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
手指 (shǒuzhǐ) = finger.
手 (hand) + 指 (point/finger).
Individual fingers: 大拇指 (thumb), 食指 (index finger), 中指 (middle finger), 无名指 (ring finger), 小指 (pinky).
割破了 = verb + resultative complement: 割 (cut) + 破 (pierce/break through) + 了 (completed).
破 as a complement indicates something was pierced or torn: 割破 (cut through skin), 碰破 (knock and scrape), 戳破 (poke and pierce).
脚趾 (jiǎozhǐ) = toe — same 趾 character but with the foot radical instead of hand.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me duele el pecho un poco.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ de xiōngbù yǒu yìdiǎn tòng.
cards.grammarNote:
胸部 (xiōngbù) = chest (with 部 suffix meaning 'area/region').
胸 alone is also used colloquially.
胸 contains the 月 (flesh radical) — shared with 腿 (leg), 胃 (stomach), 脑 (brain), 腰 (waist), 背 (back).
感到 (gǎndào) = to feel / sense.
感 (feel) + 到 (arrive at/reach) — together meaning 'sensation reaches one'.
Similar: 看到 (see), 听到 (hear), 闻到 (smell).
疼痛 (téngtòng) = pain (more formal than 疼 alone).
Both characters mean pain; combining them gives a stronger or more clinical tone.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me duele mucho la espalda.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ de bèi hěn tòng.
cards.grammarNote:
背 (bèi) = back (body).
Also pronounced bēi meaning 'to carry on one's back': 背包 (backpack).
The tone changes the meaning.
背 contains the 月 (flesh) radical at the bottom, shared with other body-part characters.
The sentence opens with a time clause: 坐了一天 (having sat for a whole day) + main clause (my back hurts).
This fronted time/condition clause is very natural in Chinese.
上背部 = upper back; 下背部 / 腰部 = lower back.

cards.spanishPhrase: El corazón late muy rápido.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xīnzàng tiào de hěn kuài.
cards.grammarNote:
心脏 (xīnzàng) = heart (the organ).
心 (xīn) alone = heart/mind (emotionally), used in many words: 心情 (mood), 开心 (happy), 心疼 (heartache).
脏 (zàng) = internal organ — also used in 肝脏 (liver), 肾脏 (kidney).
Note: 脏 has two pronunciations: zàng (organ) and zāng (dirty).
Same character, different tones and meanings!
对…有好处 = 'be good for…' — a fixed pattern: 运动对心脏有好处 (exercise is good for the heart).

cards.spanishPhrase: Me duele el estómago hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīntiān wèi hěn tòng.
cards.grammarNote:
The vocabulary word is 胃 (wèi) = stomach (the organ), but the example phrase uses 肚子 (dùzi) = belly/abdomen — the more colloquial term for stomachaches.
胃 has the 月 (flesh) radical at the bottom.
胃口 (wèikǒu) = appetite (literally 'stomach mouth').
肚子 = 肚 (belly, also with 月 radical) + 子 (diminutive suffix).
肚子疼 is the everyday phrase for stomachache.
Note: no verb 'is' is needed — body part + 疼 directly expresses pain.

cards.spanishPhrase: Tengo los músculos muy cansados.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ de jīròu hěn lèi.
cards.grammarNote:
肌肉 (jīròu) = muscle.
Both 肌 and 肉 contain or represent the flesh/meat concept.
肉 alone means 'meat/flesh' and is also the radical (月) found in most body-part characters: 脑 (brain), 背 (back), 胸 (chest), 腿 (leg), 腰 (waist).
酸 (suān) = sore/achy (the muscle-soreness feeling), literally 'sour'.
In Chinese, sore muscles feel 'sour': 肌肉酸 or 腿酸.
This is different from 疼 (sharp pain) or 痛 (aching pain).
锻炼后 = after exercise — 后 (hòu) as a standalone time word means 'after/afterwards'.

cards.spanishPhrase: Me duele la cintura al sentarme.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zuò zhe de shíhou yāo hěn tòng.
cards.grammarNote:
腰 (yāo) = waist/lower back.
Contains the 月 (flesh) radical.
腰部 (yāobù) = the waist area (more formal).
扭腰 = to twist/strain one's waist.
扭 (niǔ) = to twist, also used in 扭伤 (sprain): 扭伤了脚踝 (sprained the ankle).
The 了 here signals completion: he twisted his waist (and that's what happened).
This is the action-completion 了, placed between verb and object when the object follows: 扭了腰 (twisted the waist).

cards.spanishPhrase: Tienes la piel muy suave.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ de pífū hěn ruǎn.
cards.grammarNote:
皮肤 (pífū) = skin.
皮 (pí) = skin/leather/hide (outer layer); 肤 (fū) = skin (bodily).
皮 alone is used in: 皮鞋 (leather shoes), 皮包 (leather bag).
多 + verb = 'do more of': 多喝水 (drink more water), 多休息 (rest more), 多运动 (exercise more).
The opposite is 少 (shǎo): 少吃糖 (eat less sugar).
The sentence has no connecting word between 多喝水 and 保持皮肤健康 — in Chinese, two clauses can follow each other with an implied 'so that/to': drink more water [in order to] keep skin healthy.

cards.spanishPhrase: El médico analizó mi sangre.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Yīshēng jiǎnchá le wǒ de xuèyè.
cards.grammarNote:
血 (xuè/xiě) = blood — the everyday word.
血液 (xuèyè) is the formal/medical term (literally 'blood fluid'), used in clinical contexts.
Note: 血 has two pronunciations — xuè (formal/medical, as used here) and xiě (colloquial).
Both are correct.
抽血 (chōu xuè) = draw blood (literally 'extract blood').
抽 means to draw/extract: 抽烟 (smoke — draw smoke), 抽奖 (draw a prize).
一些 = some/a few (used for uncountable or vague quantities).

cards.spanishPhrase: El cerebro necesita descanso.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Dànǎo xūyào xiūxi.
cards.grammarNote:
大脑 (dànǎo) = brain (formal).
脑子 (nǎozi) = brain (colloquial, with 子 suffix).
脑 contains the 月 (flesh) radical — as do most internal organ/body characters.
动动 is a reduplicated verb — reduplicating a verb in Chinese softens the action or implies doing it 'a little bit/casually': 动动脑子 (use your brain a bit / get your brain moving), 看看 (take a look), 想想 (think about it).
大脑 literally = 'big brain' — 大 + 脑.
Compare 小脑 (xiǎonǎo) = cerebellum (literally 'small brain').

cards.spanishPhrase: El médico revisó su cráneo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Yīshēng jiǎnchá le tā de tóugǔ.
cards.grammarNote:
头骨 (tóugǔ) = skull.
头 (head) + 骨 (bone).
骨 alone = bone, used in: 骨头 (bone, with suffix), 骨折 (fracture — 'bone break'), 骨架 (skeleton/frame).
没有 (méiyǒu) + noun = 'do not have / there is no': 没有骨折 (no fracture).
没有 is also the negation of past actions: 他没有来 (he did not come).
骨折 = fracture: 骨 (bone) + 折 (snap/break).
Also used as a verb: 骨折了 (suffered a fracture).

cards.spanishPhrase: Me duele la pierna izquierda.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ de zuǒ tuǐ hěn tòng.
cards.grammarNote:
腿 (tuǐ) = leg.
Contains the 月 (flesh) radical — shared by many body-part characters.
腿 refers to the whole leg; 大腿 (thigh) and 小腿 (lower leg/calf) specify parts.
Chinese doesn't distinguish singular/plural — 腿 can mean 'leg' or 'legs'.
Context (or measure words) clarifies: 一条腿 (one leg) — 条 (tiáo) is the measure word for long, flexible things.
累 (lèi) = tired (physically fatigued).
Different from 困 (kùn, sleepy).
After exercise: legs feel 酸 (achy/sore) or 累 (tired/fatigued).

cards.spanishPhrase: Me duelen los pies de caminar.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zǒulù zǒu duō le jiǎo hěn tòng.
cards.grammarNote:
脚 (jiǎo) = foot/feet.
Contains the 足 (foot) radical.
Related: 脚趾 (toe), 脚踝 (ankle), 脚跟 (heel).
The phrase uses a degree complement with 得 (de): 走路走得我的脚疼 = 'walked to the degree that my feet hurt'.
Pattern: Verb + 得 + result/degree.
When a verb has an object, repeat the verb: 走路走得… Measure word: 一只脚 (one foot), 一双脚 (a pair of feet).

cards.spanishPhrase: Me golpeé el dedo del pie.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ de jiǎozhǐ zhuàng dào le.
cards.grammarNote:
脚趾 (jiǎozhǐ) = toe.
脚 (foot) + 趾 (toe).
趾 contains the 足 (foot) radical.
Compare 手指 (shǒuzhǐ, finger) — 指 has the 手 (hand) radical instead.
碰到 (pèngdào) = to bump into / hit against.
碰 (bump) + 到 (reach/arrive at, resultative complement).
The 到 signals the action reached its target.
了 at the sentence end signals a new situation or change: 'something has happened' — she stubbed her toe (and now there's a result).

cards.spanishPhrase: La articulación de la rodilla me duele.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xīgài de guānjié hěn tòng.
cards.grammarNote:
关节 (guānjié) = joint.
关 = close/connect; 节 = section/segment.
Together: a connecting section = joint.
节 also appears in 季节 (season) and 节日 (holiday/festival).
膝 (xī) = knee — giving 膝关节 (knee joint) and 膝盖 (kneecap).
膝 contains the 月 (flesh) radical.
肿了 = has become swollen.
肿 (zhǒng) = swollen; 了 signals the new state.
Common with injuries: 肿了 (swollen up), 破了 (broken/cut), 瘀了 (bruised).

cards.spanishPhrase: Puedo ver la montaña desde aquí.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ cóng zhèlǐ kěyǐ kànjiàn shān.
cards.grammarNote:
看见 (kànjiàn) = to see / catch sight of.
看 (kàn) = to look/watch (the action); 见 (jiàn) = to perceive/encounter (the result).
Together: the act of looking + the result of seeing.
看不见 = cannot see — the potential negative form: Verb + 不 + complement.
Compare 看得见 (can see).
This pattern: V + 得/不 + complement expresses potential ability.
眼镜 (yǎnjìng) = glasses — 眼 (eye) + 镜 (mirror/lens).
Compare 眼睛 (yǎnjing, eyes) — the characters look similar but are different!

cards.spanishPhrase: Puedo oír música en la calle.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zài jiē shang tīngjiàn le yīnyuè.
cards.grammarNote:
听见 (tīngjiàn) = to hear.
Parallel to 看见 (see) — 听 (listen, the action) + 见 (perceive, the result).
The 见 result complement makes these sense verbs mean 'successfully perceived'.
能 (néng) = can, expresses physical/situational capability.
Compare: 会 (huì) = can, learned skill or prediction; 可以 (kěyǐ) = may, permitted or possible.
吗 (ma) at sentence end turns a statement into a yes/no question — the simplest question formation in Chinese.

cards.spanishPhrase: Puedo oler la comida desde afuera.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我在外面就能闻到饭的香味。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zài wàimiàn jiù néng wén dào fàn de xiāngwèi.
cards.grammarNote:
闻 (wén) = to smell (sniff/detect an odor).
闻到 = to smell successfully (到 as resultative complement indicating the sensory result was achieved).
Compare 看到 (see), 听到 (hear), 感到 (feel).
闻 contains the 耳 (ear) radical inside 门 (gate) — the original meaning was 'to hear'; in modern Chinese it shifted to mean 'smell', while 听 took over 'hear'.
味道 (wèidào) = flavor/smell/taste.
味 = taste/flavor; 道 = way/path (extended meaning: quality/essence).
味道 covers both smell and taste in Chinese.

cards.spanishPhrase: Quiero saborear este plato nuevo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiǎng pǐncháng zhè dào xīn cài.
cards.grammarNote:
尝 (cháng) = to taste (everyday, casual).
The more formal term is 品尝 (pǐncháng), used in culinary or literary contexts — avoid it in everyday speech.
我尝一下 = let me have a taste.
一下 after a verb softens the action and suggests doing it briefly: 看一下 (take a quick look), 等一下 (wait a moment), 试一下 (give it a try).
尝一尝 is an alternative reduplication pattern with the same meaning: 尝 (taste) + 一 + 尝 → taste a little.

cards.spanishPhrase: No toques ese objeto caliente.
cards.chinesePhrase: 不要触摸那个热的东西。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Bú yào chùmō nà gè rè de dōngxi.
cards.grammarNote:
触摸 (chùmō) = to touch (formal/written).
In everyday speech, 碰 (pèng) or 摸 (mō) are more common: 碰 = bump/touch accidentally or lightly; 摸 = feel/stroke (more deliberate).
不要 (bù yào) = don't (prohibition).
Softer than 别 (bié) in some contexts, but both work: 别碰那个 / 不要碰那个 (don't touch that).
不要 literally = 'do not want to' but functions as an imperative negative.
那个 = that (thing).
那 (that) + 个 (general measure word).

cards.spanishPhrase: Parpadeé muchas veces seguidas.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ liánxù zhǎ le hěn duō cì yǎn.
cards.grammarNote:
眨眼 (zhǎyǎn) = to blink.
眨 (zhǎ) = to blink; contains the 目 (eye) radical.
眨了眨 is the verb + 了 + verb reduplication pattern, conveying a brief, casual, or single occurrence of the action: 'blinked (once/a little)'.
Compare 看了看 (took a look), 笑了笑 (smiled briefly).
一眨眼 (yī zhǎ yǎn) = 'in the blink of an eye' — an idiom meaning 'in an instant': 一眨眼就不见了 (gone in the blink of an eye).

cards.spanishPhrase: Sudo mucho cuando hago ejercicio.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我运动的时候很容易出汗。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ yùndòng de shíhou hěn róngyì chūhàn.
cards.grammarNote:
出汗 (chūhàn) = to sweat (literally 'emit sweat').
出 (chū) = exit/emit; 汗 (hàn) = sweat.
This 'emit body substance' pattern is common: 流血 (bleed — flow blood), 流泪 (shed tears — flow tears), 流鼻涕 (have a runny nose).
运动时 = when exercising.
时 (shí) added after a verb/noun = 'when (doing)': 吃饭时 (when eating), 睡觉时 (when sleeping).
In 出很多汗, the object 汗 is split from the compound 出汗 by the quantity 很多 — this splitting is normal when quantifying the object.

cards.spanishPhrase: La herida empezó a sangrar.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Shāngkǒu kāishǐ liúxuè le.
cards.grammarNote:
流血 (liúxuè) = to bleed (literally 'flow blood').
流 = flow; 血 = blood.
The same pattern as 出汗 (sweat) and 流泪 (shed tears).
在 (zài) before a verb = ongoing action (like '-ing' in English): 在流血 = is bleeding.
This is one of several ways to mark ongoing action: 在V, 正在V, or V着 (zhe).
手指 = finger — 手 (hand) + 指 (point/finger).
The 指 character itself can mean 'to point' as a verb.

cards.spanishPhrase: Siento frío esta mañana.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīntiān zǎoshang gǎnjué hěn lěng.
cards.grammarNote:
感觉 (gǎnjué) = to feel / feeling / sensation.
Can be a verb ('I feel…') or noun ('a feeling'): 我感觉累 (I feel tired) / 这种感觉很好 (this feeling is good).
Compare: 感觉 (general feeling/sensation), 感到 (feel/sense something specific, often emotional), 觉得 (juéde, think/feel — often expressing opinion or mild sensation).
累 (lèi) = tired/fatigued.
Different from 困 (kùn) = sleepy.
After physical exertion, use 累: 我很累 (I'm tired).

cards.spanishPhrase: Necesito pensar antes de responder.
cards.chinesePhrase: 回答之前我需要思考一下。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Huídá zhīqián wǒ xūyào sīkǎo yīxià.
cards.grammarNote:
思考 (sīkǎo) = to think/reflect (more deliberate than casual thinking).
Compare: 想 (xiǎng) = think/want (casual, everyday); 思考 = think deeply/consider carefully.
说话前 = before speaking.
Time expressions with 前 (before) and 后 (after) come before the main clause: 说话前先思考 (before speaking, first think).
先 (xiān) = first/before anything else.
Often paired with 再 (zài, then): 先想，再说 (first think, then speak).

cards.spanishPhrase: Por favor, mueve la silla a la derecha.
cards.chinesePhrase: 请把椅子向右移动一下。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng bǎ yǐzi xiàng yòu yídòng yīxià.
cards.grammarNote:
移动 (yídòng) = to move (an object or body part).
移 = shift/transfer; 动 = move/motion.
For moving one's own body parts in everyday speech, 动 alone is also common: 动一动手臂 (move your arm around).
慢慢 (mànmàn) = slowly.
Reduplicated adjective used as an adverb: 慢慢走 (walk slowly), 慢慢来 (take your time — a common reassurance).
Reduplication of monosyllabic adjectives typically adds a gentler, more gradual quality.
手臂 = arm: 手 (hand) + 臂 (upper arm).
The 臂 character contains the 月 flesh radical.

cards.spanishPhrase: Sus manos se ven muy huesudas.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā de shǒu kàn qǐlái hěn gǔgǎn.
cards.grammarNote:
骨感 (gǔgǎn) = bony/angular (literally 'bone-feeling').
骨 (bone) + 感 (sensation/feeling).
In modern Chinese, 骨感 can be a compliment (slender, model-like figure) or a description of being thin/bony.
看起来 (kàn qǐlái) = to look / appear.
A very useful fixed phrase: 看起来很累 (looks tired), 看起来很年轻 (looks young).
The 起来 directional complement adds the meaning of 'upon looking/in appearance'.
骨 (gǔ) appears in: 骨头 (bone), 头骨 (skull), 骨折 (fracture), 骨架 (skeleton).

cards.spanishPhrase: Su perro es muy peludo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā de gǒu fēicháng duō máo.
cards.grammarNote:
多毛 (duō máo) = hairy (literally 'lots of hair/fur').
毛 (máo) = body hair, fur, feathers (shorter/finer hair, unlike 发 which is head hair).
毛 also means 'cent' (money) in colloquial speech.
Distinguish: 头发 (tóufa) = head hair; 毛发 (máofà) = hair/fur (general); 眉毛 (éyebrow hair); 睫毛 (eyelashes).
很多 typically means 'a lot of' — here 很多毛 = a lot of body hair, describing the state as having much fur/hair.

cards.spanishPhrase: Esta almohada es muy suave.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhège zhěntou hěn ruǎn.
cards.grammarNote:
软 (ruǎn) = soft.
Opposite: 硬 (yìng) = hard.
Both describe textures and also figurative qualities: 软件 (software — 'soft piece'), 硬件 (hardware — 'hard piece').
婴儿 (yīng'ér) = infant/baby.
The apostrophe in the romanization marks a syllable boundary (ying + er), preventing yinger from being read as yin + ger.
皮肤 = skin.
Note that 很 before an adjective in a descriptive sentence is not truly 'very' — it's a weak intensifier that sounds more natural than a bare adjective.
皮肤软 (without 很) would imply comparison.

cards.spanishPhrase: Tengo los pies muy doloridos hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīntiān de jiǎo hěn suāntòng.
cards.grammarNote:
酸痛 (suāntòng) = sore/achy (the dull post-exercise feeling).
酸 = sour/achy; 痛 = pain.
Together: the 'sour-aching' sensation typical of muscle soreness.
Three key pain words: 疼 (téng, general hurt/ache), 痛 (tòng, sharper or more intense pain), 酸痛 (suāntòng, the achy-sour soreness after exercise).
运动完 = after finishing exercise.
完 as a resultative complement: 运动完 = finished exercising.
This time expression fronts the sentence naturally in Chinese.

cards.spanishPhrase: Gira a la izquierda en esa calle.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zài nà tiáo jiē xiàng zuǒbiān zhuǎn.
cards.grammarNote:
左 (zuǒ) = left; 右 (yòu) = right.
A mnemonic: 左 has 工 (work) on the right side of the character — 'most people work with their right hand, so the work-side is the opposite of left.' Made-up but memorable!
举起 (jǔqǐ) = raise/lift up.
举 = raise; 起 = directional complement (upward motion).
左手 (left hand), 右手 (right hand), 左脚 (left foot), 右脚 (right foot) — direction + body part is the standard pattern.

cards.spanishPhrase: La tienda está al lado derecho.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Shāngdiàn zài yòubiān nà yī cè.
cards.grammarNote:
把 (bǎ) is the disposal/manipulation marker — it moves the object before the verb to emphasize how it is handled/affected: 把头转向右边 (turn the head toward the right).
把 sentences require a specific, actionable result.
转向 (zhuǎn xiàng) = turn toward.
转 = turn/rotate; 向 = toward/facing.
右边 = right side: 右 (right) + 边 (side).
Similarly: 左边 (left side), 上边 (top), 下边 (bottom).
把 + object + verb pattern is essential in Chinese for expressing deliberate manipulation of an object.

cards.spanishPhrase: Vivo en la parte superior del edificio.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zhù zài lóu de shàngbù.
cards.grammarNote:
上 (shàng) = upper/above; 下 (xià) = lower/below.
As prefixes for body areas: 上背部 (upper back), 下背部 (lower back), 上肢 (upper limbs), 下肢 (lower limbs).
背部 = the back region.
部 (bù) = area/part/section — added to body parts to specify the region: 胸部 (chest area), 腰部 (waist area), 腿部 (leg area).
疼痛 (téngtòng) = pain.
More formal than 疼 alone, often used in medical contexts.
Both 疼 and 痛 mean pain — combining them intensifies or formalizes the meaning.

cards.spanishPhrase: El dolor está en la parte inferior.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Téngtòng zài shēntǐ de xiàbù.
cards.grammarNote:
下 (xià) = lower/below.
Opposite of 上 (shàng, upper).
下部 = lower part/section.
Interestingly, the example phrase uses 腰部 (waist area) rather than 下背部 (lower back) — in Chinese, lower back pain is typically described as 腰疼 or 腰痛 rather than specifying 'lower'.
腰 naturally refers to the lumbar region.
腰部 = waist/lower back area.
部 adds the sense of 'the region of'.
Pattern: body part + 部 = the [body part] area: 腰部 (waist region), 肩部 (shoulder region), 颈部 (neck region).

cards.spanishPhrase: Necesitas cuidar tu cuerpo cada día.
cards.chinesePhrase: 你每天都要照顾好自己的身体。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ měitiān dōu yào zhàogù hǎo zìjǐ de shēntǐ.
cards.grammarNote:
身体 (shēntǐ) = body (physical health and body).
身 = torso/self; 体 = body/form.
Used in greetings: 身体好吗？ (How is your health?).
Compare 身材 (shēncái) = figure/physique (shape of body).
爱护 (àihù) = take care of / cherish and protect.
爱 (love) + 护 (protect).
Stronger than just 照顾 (zhàogù, look after) — implies both caring and guarding.
身 alone appears in: 身高 (height), 身份 (identity), 身边 (by one's side).

cards.spanishPhrase: El cuerpo humano es muy complejo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Rénlèi de shēntǐ fēicháng fùzá.
cards.grammarNote:
人类 (rénlèi) = humankind/humanity.
人 (person) + 类 (type/category).
人类的 = 'of humanity' / 'human (adj.)'.
Compare: 人 alone (a person), 人们 (rénmen, people in general), 人类 (humankind as a species).
大脑 = brain: 大 (big) + 脑 (brain).
脑 contains the 月 (flesh) radical.
The character 脑 can be broken down as flesh (月) + the brain structure on top.
神奇 (shénqí) = amazing/miraculous.
神 = spirit/divine; 奇 = strange/wondrous.
Together: wonderfully astonishing.
零 is used in numbers like 一零五 (105) to represent an internal zero, as well as for reading phone numbers and ID numbers digit by digit.
cards.spanishPhrase: Mi puntuación fue cero hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīntiān de fēnshù shì líng.

cards.grammarNote: 一 changes tone depending on what follows: yí before 4th-tone syllables (一个 yí gè), yì before 1st/2nd/3rd-tone syllables (一天 yì tiān).
cards.spanishPhrase: Tengo solo un hermano.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zhǐyǒu yí gè gēgē.

cards.grammarNote: 二 is used in counting and in compound numbers (十二, 二十). When saying 'two [things]' before a measure word, use 两 instead.
cards.spanishPhrase: Hay dos opciones en el menú.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Càidān shang yǒu liǎng gè xuǎnxiàng.

cards.grammarNote: 两 replaces 二 before measure words and time words: 两个人 (two people), 两天 (two days), 两年 (two years). Never say 二个.
cards.spanishPhrase: Compré dos manzanas en la tienda.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我在商店买了两个苹果。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zài shāngdiàn mǎi le liǎng gè píngguǒ.

cards.spanishPhrase: Tengo tres clases hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīntiān yǒu sān jié kè.
cards.grammarNote:
三 (sān) is one of the most compositionally important digits in Chinese, forming the base for 三十 (sānshí, thirty), 三百 (sānbǎi, three hundred), and countless compounds.
When counting objects, always pair it with a measure word: 三本书 (sān běn shū, three books), 三杯茶 (sān bēi chá, three cups of tea).
Culturally, three is considered a lively, auspicious odd number, and phrases like 三人行 (sān rén xíng, three people walking together) carry a Confucian flavor.
The character visually depicts three horizontal strokes—one for each unit—making it among the easiest hanzi to remember.

cards.spanishPhrase: Vivimos en el cuarto piso.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen zhù zài sì lóu.
cards.grammarNote:
四 (sì) is notorious in Chinese culture because its pronunciation is nearly identical to 死 (sǐ, to die), differing only in tone; this association makes four widely avoided in floor numbers, phone numbers, and gifts.
As a modifier, use the general measure word 个 (gè) for people and generic items: 四个人 (sì gè rén, four people).
In the example phrase 四楼 (sì lóu), no measure word is needed because 楼 already functions as an ordinal floor counter.
Remember: in formal writing, 4 is rendered 四, never substituted with another character.

cards.spanishPhrase: Necesito cinco minutos más.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ hái xūyào wǔ fēnzhōng.
cards.grammarNote:
五 (wǔ) carries strong positive cultural symbolism—five directions, five elements (五行 wǔxíng), and five blessings (五福 wǔfú) are central to traditional Chinese cosmology.
The measure word for minutes is 分钟 (fēnzhōng): 五分钟 (wǔ fēnzhōng, five minutes).
Note that when 五 precedes 十 to form 五十 (fifty), the compound follows the tens-pattern of multiplying: digit × 十.
Visually, 五 resembles an ancient pictograph of five fingers, aiding memory.

cards.spanishPhrase: Hay seis personas en la mesa.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhuōzi pángbiān yǒu liù gè rén.
cards.grammarNote:
六 (liù) is considered lucky in Chinese culture because its pronunciation rhymes with 流 (liú, to flow), symbolizing smooth fortune—hence the popular phrase 六六大顺 (liù liù dà shùn, everything goes smoothly).
For counting people at a table as in the example, use 个 (gè): 六个人 (liù gè rén).
The phrase 桌子旁边有六个人 demonstrates the existential construction 有 (yǒu) + number + measure word + noun to express 'there are X things.' Phone numbers and business addresses frequently feature 六 for its auspicious ring.

cards.spanishPhrase: La semana tiene siete días.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Yí gè xīngqī yǒu qī tiān.
cards.grammarNote:
七 (qī) is the number of days in a week, reflected directly in the example 一个星期有七天 (yī gè xīngqī yǒu qī tiān, a week has seven days).
The measure word for weeks is 个星期 (gè xīngqī) or 周 (zhōu); for days within a week, no extra measure word is needed since 天 (tiān) itself serves as a time-unit noun.
In traditional culture, 七夕 (Qīxī, the seventh day of the seventh lunar month) is China's Valentine's Day, celebrating the legend of the Cowherd and Weaver Girl.
The shape of 七 is sometimes compared to a reversed numeral 7 in Western script.

cards.spanishPhrase: Empiezo a trabajar a las ocho.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ bā diǎn kāishǐ gōngzuò.
cards.grammarNote:
八 (bā) is the luckiest number in Chinese culture because it sounds like 发 (fā) in 发财 (fācái, to become wealthy); the 2008 Beijing Olympics deliberately opened on 08/08/08 at 8:08 PM for this reason.
Time expressions with clock hours omit a measure word: 八点 (bā diǎn, eight o'clock), where 点 acts as the clock-hour counter.
The example 我八点开始工作 (wǒ bā diǎn kāishǐ gōngzuò) shows a time word placed directly before the verb without a preposition.
The character's two symmetric strokes splaying outward look like open arms welcoming good fortune.

cards.spanishPhrase: El niño tiene nueve años.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhège xiǎohái jiǔ suì le.
cards.grammarNote:
九 (jiǔ) sounds identical to 久 (jiǔ, long-lasting/forever), so it symbolizes longevity and is popular in wedding contexts.
Age is expressed in Chinese with the noun 岁 (suì) following the number, not a verb-preposition: 九岁 (jiǔ suì, nine years old).
The full pattern is Subject + number + 岁 + 了 to mark a completed state: 这个小孩九岁了 (zhège xiǎohái jiǔ suì le).
Unlike English, no linking verb 'is' is needed; the number and 岁 together form the predicate.

cards.spanishPhrase: Compré diez naranjas en el mercado.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我在市场买了十个橙子。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zài shìchǎng mǎi le shí gè chéngzi.
cards.grammarNote:
十 (shí) is the structural backbone of the Chinese decimal system: all tens are formed by digit + 十, and teens by 十 + digit, making number formation completely regular above nine.
The example uses the measure word 个 (gè) with 橙子 (chéngzi, oranges): 十个橙子 (shí gè chéngzi).
The character 十 is a simple cross (+), historically representing a tally mark for completeness.
In Mandarin, 十 also appears in 十分 (shífēn, very/extremely), a common adverb meaning 'ten parts' worth of intensity.

cards.spanishPhrase: El autobús llega a las once.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Gōnggòng qìchē shí yī diǎn dào.
cards.grammarNote:
十一 (shíyī) follows the regular pattern: 十 (ten) + 一 (one) = eleven, no irregularities or special words needed, unlike many European languages.
In time expressions, 十一点 (shíyī diǎn, 11 o'clock) uses 点 as the hour marker.
Notably, 十一 is also the colloquial name for China's National Day holiday (国庆节 Guóqìng Jié) on October 1st, which often extends into a week-long 'Golden Week.' The predictable compounding of Chinese numbers makes arithmetic and time-telling significantly easier for learners than in English.

cards.spanishPhrase: Hay doce meses en un año.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Yī nián yǒu shí èr gè yuè.
cards.grammarNote:
十二 (shíèr) follows the same additive pattern: 十 (ten) + 二 (two).
The example 一年有十二个月 (yī nián yǒu shí'èr gè yuè, a year has twelve months) uses the measure word 个 (gè) with 月 (yuè, month).
Note the use of 有 (yǒu) in the existential/possessive frame 'X has Y.' Twelve also appears in the Chinese zodiac cycle (十二生肖 shíèr shēngxiào), reinforcing its cultural prominence.
Compound numbers follow a straightforward pattern: 二十一 (21), 二十二 (22), etc.
cards.spanishPhrase: Mi hermana tiene veinte años.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jiějie èr shí suì le.

cards.spanishPhrase: Estudié durante treinta minutos.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xuéxí le sān shí fēnzhōng.
cards.grammarNote:
三十 (sānshí) is formed by 三 (three) × 十 (ten) in a multiplicative pattern that applies consistently to all tens: 四十, 五十, 六十, etc.
The measure word for minutes is 分钟 (fēnzhōng): 三十分钟 (sānshí fēnzhōng, thirty minutes), though in clock time, 三十分 (sānshí fēn) or the shorthand 半 (bàn, half) is used interchangeably—e.g., 八点三十 or 八点半 both mean 8:30.
Durations of studied time use the verb + time-duration pattern: 学习了三十分钟 (xuéxí le sānshí fēnzhōng, studied for thirty minutes), with 了 marking completion.

cards.spanishPhrase: Hay cincuenta estudiantes en la clase.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Bān lǐ yǒu wǔ shí gè xuéshēng.
cards.grammarNote:
五十 (wǔshí) is five tens, following the same multiplicative pattern as all Chinese decade numbers.
When expressing quantities of people in a classroom, 个 (gè) is the appropriate measure word: 五十个学生 (wǔshí gè xuésheng, fifty students).
The location word 班里 (bān lǐ, inside the class) illustrates how Chinese uses localizers like 里 (lǐ, inside) after nouns to indicate a container or group.
Fifty also appears in the phrase 五十开 (wǔshí kāi, fifty-fifty), popular in Mandarin slang.
For numbers like 101, insert 零: 一百零一. For 110, say 一百一十.
cards.spanishPhrase: Este libro cuesta cien pesos.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhè běn shū yī bǎi kuài qián.

cards.spanishPhrase: Gané mil dólares este mes.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我这个月赚了一千块钱。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zhège yuè zhuàn le yī qiān kuài qián.
cards.grammarNote:
一千 (yīqiān, one thousand) introduces a new magnitude unit: 千 (qiān).
Chinese numbers use units of 万 (wàn, ten thousand) as the next major grouping after 千, so large numbers are segmented differently from English—one million is 一百万 (yībǎi wàn, one hundred ten-thousands).
Money amounts often use 块 (kuài) colloquially for 元 (yuán): 一千块钱 (yīqiān kuài qián, one thousand yuan).
Note that 一 must be included before 千 (unlike in English where 'thousand' alone suffices), and this rule applies to 百 (bǎi) as well in most formal contexts.
Chinese groups large numbers by ten-thousands (万), not thousands. 100,000 = 十万; 1,000,000 = 一百万.
cards.spanishPhrase: La ciudad tiene diez mil habitantes.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhège chéngshì yǒu yī wàn rén.

cards.grammarNote: 亿 = 100,000,000 (one hundred million). The Chinese number system uses 亿 as its largest named unit in everyday speech.
cards.spanishPhrase: El país tiene cien millones de personas.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhège guójiā yǒu yī yì rénkǒu.

cards.grammarNote: 半 follows a number and measure word: 一个半小时 (one and a half hours), 半个小时 (half an hour). For time on the clock, 半 means 'half past': 三点半 (3:30).
cards.spanishPhrase: Solo comí la mitad del pastel.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zhǐ chī le bàn gè dàngāo.

cards.grammarNote: Ordinals are formed by placing 第 before a number: 第一 (first), 第二 (second). Used for rankings and order, not for dates (dates use 号/日).
cards.spanishPhrase: Llegué primero a la clase hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīntiān dì yī gè dào jiàoshì.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ella llegó en segundo lugar.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā shì dì èr gè dàodá de.
cards.grammarNote:
第二 (dì èr) uses the ordinal prefix 第 (dì), which attaches to any cardinal number to form an ordinal: 第一 (first), 第二 (second), 第三 (third), and so on without limit.
In the example 她是第二个到达的, 个 is the measure word and 的 nominalizes the verb phrase 到达 (dào dá, to arrive).
This 是……的 construction emphasizes circumstances of a completed action.
Ordinals with 第 are used for rankings, sequences, and floor numbers in formal speech.

cards.spanishPhrase: Él terminó en tercer lugar.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tā dé le dì sān míng.
cards.grammarNote:
第三 (dì sān) follows the same 第 + cardinal pattern.
In competition contexts, 第三名 (dì sān míng, third place) uses 名 as the measure word for ranked positions.
The example 他得了第三名 uses the verb 得 (dé, to obtain/receive), which is the standard verb for 'to place/earn' in competition results.
An alternative construction is 他得到了第三名 with the resultative complement 到 (dào) for emphasis.
Chinese competition rankings prefer 第 + number + 名 over Western-style 'X place.'

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Qué hora es ahora?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xiànzài jǐ diǎn le?
cards.grammarNote:
现在 (xiànzài) literally means 'present existence': 现 (xiàn, present/current) + 在 (zài, to be at/exist).
It functions as a time adverb placed before the verb or at the start of a sentence.
In questions about time, 现在几点了？(xiànzài jǐ diǎn le?, What time is it now?) uses 几 (jǐ, how many, for numbers under 10) and 点 (diǎn, o'clock), with the particle 了 signaling a change of state.
现在 can also mark contrast with past or future: 我现在不想吃 (I don't want to eat right now) sets a temporal frame for the predicate.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Qué planes tienes hoy?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ jīntiān yǒu shénme jìhuà?
cards.grammarNote:
今天 (jīntiān) is built from 今 (jīn, current/this) + 天 (tiān, day/sky), literally 'this day.' Time words in Chinese function as adverbs and are typically placed after the subject but before the verb, or at the very start of the sentence: 你今天有什么计划？(nǐ jīntiān yǒu shénme jìhuà?).
The parallel structure today/tomorrow/yesterday (今天/明天/昨天) uses the same suffix 天, making the trio easy to learn together.
今 also appears in 今年 (jīnnián, this year) and 今晚 (jīnwǎn, tonight), all sharing the 'current' semantic.

cards.spanishPhrase: Ayer fui al mercado con mamá.
cards.chinesePhrase: 昨天我和妈妈去了市场。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zuótiān wǒ hé māma qù le shìchǎng.
cards.grammarNote:
昨天 (zuótiān) combines 昨 (zuó, yesterday) + 天 (tiān, day); 昨 is rarely used outside this compound in modern Mandarin.
Past actions in Chinese do not require verb conjugation; instead, the particle 了 (le) or time words like 昨天 signal past tense: 昨天我和妈妈去了市场 (zuótiān wǒ hé māma qù le shìchǎng).
Note the conjunction 和 (hé, and/with) connecting co-participants.
昨 appears in the literary compound 昨日 (zuórì, yesterday), more formal than 昨天 and common in written Chinese.

cards.spanishPhrase: Mañana tenemos una reunión importante.
cards.chinesePhrase: 明天我们有一个重要的会议。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Míngtiān wǒmen yǒu yí gè zhòngyào de huìyì.
cards.grammarNote:
明天 (míngtiān) combines 明 (míng, bright/next) + 天 (tiān, day).
The character 明 itself is composed of 日 (rì, sun) + 月 (yuè, moon), representing brilliance or the dawning of the next day.
Future events in Chinese require no special verb form; context or time words like 明天 convey futurity: 明天我们有一个重要的会议 (míngtiān wǒmen yǒu yīgè zhòngyào de huìyì).
明 also extends to 明年 (míngnián, next year) and 明白 (míngbai, to understand), both carrying senses of clarity or what comes next.

cards.spanishPhrase: Pasado mañana empieza mis vacaciones.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Hòutiān wǒ de jiàqī kāishǐ le.
cards.grammarNote:
后天 (hòutiān) uses 后 (hòu, after/behind) + 天 (tiān, day), literally 'the day that comes after.' Chinese temporal vocabulary maps spatial metaphors onto time consistently: 后 (behind/future) and 前 (before/past).
The system is perfectly regular: 前天 (qiántiān, day before yesterday), 昨天, 今天, 明天, 后天—five days named by position relative to now.
For the week level, the same logic extends: 上周 (last week), 这周 (this week), 下周 (next week).
No equivalent single word exists for 'the day after tomorrow' in most European languages, making 后天 a uniquely efficient term.

cards.spanishPhrase: Anteayer llovió todo el día.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qiántiān zhěng tiān dōu zài xià yǔ.
cards.grammarNote:
前天 (qiántiān) pairs 前 (qián, front/before) + 天 (tiān, day)—the day 'in front of' in the backward direction of past time.
Chinese uses 前 (front) for the past and 后 (back) for the future, the opposite of how English speakers often conceptualize time.
The example 前天整天都在下雨 uses the pattern 整天 (zhěng tiān, the whole day) + 都 (dōu, all/entirely) + 在下雨 (zài xià yǔ, was raining), with 在 indicating a continuous action in progress.
都 here emphasizes the totality of the duration.

cards.spanishPhrase: Este año quiero aprender chino.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīnnián wǒ xiǎng xué Hànyǔ.
cards.grammarNote:
今年 (jīnnián) follows the same 今 (jīn, current) root as 今天, but pairs with 年 (nián, year) instead of 天 (tiān, day).
The parallel set 去年/今年/明年 (last/this/next year) mirrors the day set with different temporal roots for each direction.
In the example 今年我想学汉语, the modal 想 (xiǎng, want to) precedes the verb 学 (xué, to study), and 汉语 (Hànyǔ, Chinese language) literally means 'Han people's language.' Note that 今年 as a time word can open the sentence or follow the subject with equal grammaticality.

cards.spanishPhrase: El año que viene me graduaré.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Míngnián wǒ jiù bìyè le.
cards.grammarNote:
明年 (míngnián) uses 明 (míng, bright/next) + 年 (nián, year).
The construction 明年我就毕业了 (míngnián wǒ jiù bìyè le) features the adverb 就 (jiù), which here expresses imminence or inevitability: 'next year I will [already] have graduated.' The particle 了 at the end signals anticipated completion.
This 就……了 pattern is very common for describing events close in time or certain to happen.
毕业 (bìyè, to graduate) literally means 'complete one's studies.'

cards.spanishPhrase: El año pasado viví en España.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qùnián wǒ zhù zài Xībānyá.
cards.grammarNote:
去年 (qùnián) uses 去 (qù, to go/past) + 年 (nián, year)—the year that has 'gone.' The example 去年我住在西班牙 uses the locative verb 住在 (zhù zài, to live in/at) + place, a key pattern for expressing residence; 在 here is part of the verb complement, not a standalone preposition.
Chinese does not require past-tense verb forms; 去年 alone makes the timeline clear.
Compare 去年 with 以前 (yǐqián, before/in the past) for less specific past references.
以前 follows a time or event reference: 吃饭以前 (before eating), 十年以前 (ten years ago). It differs from 前 which primarily means 'front' or 'previous' in compounds.
cards.spanishPhrase: Antes comía mucho más rápido.
cards.chinesePhrase: 以前我吃饭吃得快多了。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Yǐqián wǒ chīfàn chī de kuài duō le.

cards.grammarNote: 以后 can mean 'after' (吃饭以后 — after eating) or 'from now on / in the future' when used alone (以后我会努力 — I'll work hard from now on).
cards.spanishPhrase: Después del trabajo, voy al gimnasio.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Gōngzuò yǐhòu wǒ qù jiànshēnfáng.

cards.grammarNote: 早上 typically refers to early morning (roughly 6–9 am). For the broader morning period, 上午 (shàngwǔ) is used (roughly 9 am–noon).
cards.spanishPhrase: Por la mañana tomo café.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zǎoshang hē kāfēi.

cards.spanishPhrase: Almorzamos juntos al mediodía.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen zhōngwǔ yīqǐ chī wǔfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
中午 (zhōngwǔ) literally means 'middle of 午': 中 (zhōng, middle/center) + 午 (wǔ, the seventh earthly branch, corresponding to 11 AM–1 PM in traditional timekeeping).
中午 contrasts with 上午 (shàngwǔ, morning/AM) and 下午 (xiàwǔ, afternoon/PM), where 上 (above) and 下 (below) describe the sun's position relative to its noon peak.
The meal associated with 中午 is 午饭 (wǔfàn, lunch).
Note that 午 is not the same character as 五 (wǔ, five) despite identical pronunciation.

cards.spanishPhrase: Por la tarde salimos a caminar.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen xiàwǔ chūqù sànbù.
cards.grammarNote:
下午 (xiàwǔ) means 'below noon': 下 (xià, below/down) + 午 (wǔ, midday).
This spatial metaphor for time is consistent: 上午 (shàngwǔ, AM) uses 上 (above) because the sun is rising toward noon, while 下午 uses 下 because the sun descends after noon.
The example 我们下午出去散步 contains 散步 (sànbù, to take a walk), literally 'to scatter steps.' Time words like 下午 are placed before the verb phrase in standard SVO order.
In formal writing, 午后 (wǔhòu, post-noon) is a literary synonym for 下午.

cards.spanishPhrase: Por la noche veo televisión.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ wǎnshang kàn diànshì.
cards.grammarNote:
晚上 (wǎnshang) combines 晚 (wǎn, late/evening) + 上 (shàng, above/on top of), where 上 functions as a localizer suffix turning the time word into a usable adverb—compare 早上 (zǎoshang, morning), 路上 (lùshang, on the road).
The second syllable 上 is typically unstressed (neutral tone) in speech.
晚上 covers the period from roughly sunset to midnight; for the time after midnight, 深夜 (shēnyè) or 凌晨 (língchén) are used.
The example 我晚上看电视 places the time word between subject and verb, the standard position for temporal adverbs.
年 is both the measure word and noun for year: 三年 (three years), 今年 (this year). For 'two years', say 两年, not 二年.
cards.spanishPhrase: Este año tiene muchos días festivos.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīnnián yǒu hěn duō jiàrì.

cards.grammarNote: 月 means both 'month' and 'moon'. It also names the months: 一月 (January), 二月 (February), etc.
cards.spanishPhrase: Cada mes ahorro un poco de dinero.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ měi gè yuè cún yìdiǎn qián.

cards.grammarNote: 日 is used for calendar dates in formal writing (三月一日 — March 1st). In speech, 号 (hào) is more common: 三月一号.
cards.spanishPhrase: Hoy es el primer día del mes.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān shì zhège yuè de yī rì.

cards.grammarNote: 小时 is the measure noun for hours of duration: 两个小时 (two hours). For clock time, use 点: 两点 (2 o'clock). The measure word for 小时 is 个.
cards.spanishPhrase: El viaje dura dos horas.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhè duàn lǚchéng yào liǎng gè xiǎoshí.

cards.grammarNote: 分钟 is the duration noun ('X minutes'): 五分钟 (five minutes). On a clock face, 分 (fēn) alone is used: 两点十分 (2:10).
cards.spanishPhrase: Llegaré en diez minutos.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ shí fēnzhōng hòu jiù dào.

cards.grammarNote: The measure word for 秒 is 个 in casual speech, but 秒 often stands alone in time expressions: 三十秒 (thirty seconds).
cards.spanishPhrase: Solo tardé treinta segundos.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zhǐ yòng le sānshí miǎo.

cards.spanishPhrase: Últimamente no he dormido bien.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zuìjìn shuìmián bú tài hǎo.
cards.grammarNote:
最近 (zuìjìn) is composed of 最 (zuì, most/superlative) + 近 (jìn, near/close), literally 'most near [in time].' 最 is the standard superlative marker in Chinese: 最大 (zuì dà, biggest), 最好 (zuì hǎo, best).
As a time adverb, 最近 covers a vague recent window similar to English 'lately' or 'these days,' and is placed before the verb.
The example 我最近睡眠不太好 uses 不太 (bú tài, not very/not quite) as a softened negation.
最近 can also refer to the near future: 最近我要出差 (I'm going on a business trip soon).

cards.spanishPhrase: En el futuro quiero vivir en España.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jiānglái wǒ xiǎng zhù zài Xībānyá.
cards.grammarNote:
将来 (jiānglái) pairs 将 (jiāng, about to/will) + 来 (lái, to come), literally 'what is about to come.' It refers to the distant or abstract future, in contrast to 以后 (yǐhòu, after/later) which anchors the future to a specific reference point.
In the example 将来我想住在西班牙, the modal 想 (xiǎng, want to) expresses desire.
将来 often frames aspirational or life-plan statements, while 马上 (mǎshàng) or 等一下 (děng yīxià) handle the immediate future.
过去 as a noun means 'the past'. As a verb it means 'to pass by / go over': 走过去 (walk over there). Context determines the meaning.
cards.spanishPhrase: En el pasado no había internet.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Guòqù méiyǒu wǎngluò.

cards.spanishPhrase: A menudo como en ese restaurante.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我经常在那家餐厅吃饭。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīngcháng zài nà jiā cāntīng chīfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
经常 (jīngcháng) combines 经 (jīng, to pass through/undergo) + 常 (cháng, ordinary/frequent), conveying habitual regularity.
It functions as an adverb placed directly before the verb: 经常吃饭 (jīngcháng chīfàn, often eat).
Synonyms on a frequency scale include: 总是 (zǒngshì, always) > 经常 (often) > 有时 (yǒushí, sometimes) > 偶尔 (ǒu'ěr, occasionally) > 从不 (cóng bù, never).
The example 我经常在那家餐厅吃饭 uses 那家 (nà jiā, that [measure word for businesses]), where 家 is the measure word for shops and restaurants.

cards.spanishPhrase: A veces camino al trabajo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ yǒushí zǒulù qù shàngbān.
cards.grammarNote:
有时 (yǒushí) breaks down as 有 (yǒu, to have/there is) + 时 (shí, time/moment), literally 'there are times [when].' The full form is 有时候 (yǒushíhou), with 候 appended; both are interchangeable in speech.
On the frequency adverb scale, 有时 falls between 经常 (often) and 偶尔 (occasionally).
The example 我有时走路去上班 shows the directional complement pattern: verb 走路 + 去 + destination 上班 (go to work), where 去 links movement to purpose.

cards.spanishPhrase: De vez en cuando salimos a cenar.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen ǒu'ěr chūqù chī wǎnfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
偶尔 (ǒu'ěr) means 'by chance, once in a while': 偶 (ǒu, accidental/even-numbered) + 尔 (ěr, thus/so, a classical particle).
The apostrophe in the pinyin ǒu'ěr marks a syllable boundary.
偶尔 sits at the low end of the frequency scale—less than 有时 (sometimes) but more than 从不 (never).
It typically describes pleasant but infrequent activities, as in 我们偶尔出去吃晚饭 (we occasionally go out for dinner).
The literary flavor of 尔 gives 偶尔 a slightly more formal or written feel compared to 有时.

cards.spanishPhrase: Enseguida te llamo, espera un momento.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ mǎshàng gěi nǐ dǎ diànhuà.
cards.grammarNote:
马上 (mǎshàng) literally means 'on horseback': 马 (mǎ, horse) + 上 (shàng, on top of).
The idiom originates from military contexts where mounted messengers acted with urgency.
Today it functions as an adverb meaning 'immediately/right away' and is placed before the verb: 马上来 (mǎshàng lái, coming right away).
The example 我马上给你打电话 uses the pattern 给 (gěi) + recipient + verb, where 给 marks the beneficiary of the action.
Synonyms include 立刻 (lìkè) and 立即 (lìjí), which are slightly more formal.

cards.spanishPhrase: Por fin encontré mis llaves.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我终于找到我的钥匙了。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zhōngyú zhǎodào wǒ de yàoshi le.
cards.grammarNote:
终于 (zhōngyú) combines 终 (zhōng, end/finish) + 于 (yú, at/in, a classical preposition used as a suffix), conveying the sense of reaching a conclusion after waiting.
It always carries an emotional charge—relief, satisfaction, or exasperation—distinguishing it from the neutral 最后 (zuìhòu, lastly/at the end).
终于 must be placed before the verb.
The example 我终于找到我的钥匙了 uses the resultative complement 找到 (zhǎo dào, found successfully), where 到 signals successful completion of the search, and 了 confirms the new state.
刚才 refers strictly to the recent past and is always used with past-tense context. 刚 (gāng) alone means 'just / just did': 我刚吃 (I just ate).
cards.spanishPhrase: Hace un momento llegó mi amigo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ de péngyou gāngcái dào le.

cards.grammarNote: 一直 implies continuous, uninterrupted action or state: 我一直等你 (I've been waiting for you all along). It differs from 总是 (zǒngshì — always/habitually).
cards.spanishPhrase: Todo el tiempo he sabido la verdad.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ yīzhí zhīdào zhè jiàn shì.

cards.spanishPhrase: De repente empezó a llover.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Tūrán kāishǐ xià yǔ le.
cards.grammarNote:
突然 (tūrán) means 'suddenly': 突 (tū, to protrude/abrupt) + 然 (rán, thus/so, a classical suffix forming adverbs).
Many Chinese adverbs of manner end in 然: 忽然 (hūrán, all of a sudden), 当然 (dāngrán, of course), 自然 (zìrán, naturally).
突然 can function as both an adverb (before the verb) and a predicate adjective.
The example 突然开始下雨了 places 突然 at the sentence start for emphasis; 下雨 (xià yǔ, to rain) literally means 'rain falls down,' and 了 marks the onset of a new situation.
Days of the week use 星期 + number (1–6) or 星期日/天 for Sunday. Alternatively, 周 (zhōu) can replace 星期: 周一, 周二, etc. — more common in writing.
cards.spanishPhrase: El lunes tengo una reunión importante.
cards.chinesePhrase: 星期一我有一个重要的会议。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xīngqī yī wǒ yǒu yī gè zhòngyào de huìyì.

cards.spanishPhrase: El martes voy al gimnasio.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xīngqī èr wǒ qù jiànshēnfáng.
cards.grammarNote:
星期二 (xīngqī èr) uses the system where 星期 (xīngqī, week, literally 'star period') is followed by a number 1–6 for Monday through Saturday, with Sunday being 星期日 (xīngqī rì) or 星期天 (xīngqī tiān).
An alternate prefix 周 (zhōu) is equally common: 周二 (zhōu èr, Tuesday).
Chinese weekday names use pure numbers unlike English names derived from celestial bodies or Norse gods, making the system completely transparent and easy to learn systematically.

cards.spanishPhrase: El miércoles como con mi familia.
cards.chinesePhrase: 星期三我和家人一起吃饭。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xīngqī sān wǒ hé jiārén yīqǐ chīfàn.
cards.grammarNote:
星期三 (xīngqī sān) is Wednesday, the 'third star period.' An easy memory pattern: Monday = 一, Tuesday = 二, Wednesday = 三—just count up from one.
The example 星期三我和家人一起吃饭 demonstrates the co-activity pattern: Subject + 和 (hé, with) + companion + 一起 (yīqǐ, together) + verb.
Note that 一起 always follows the co-participant phrase and precedes the verb.
家人 (jiārén, family members) is a compound of 家 (jiā, family/home) + 人 (rén, people).

cards.spanishPhrase: El jueves tengo clase de chino.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xīngqī sì wǒ yǒu Zhōngwén kè.
cards.grammarNote:
星期四 (xīngqī sì) is Thursday, the fourth day of the Chinese week (which begins on Monday).
The avoidance of four due to its homophony with 死 (sǐ, death) applies in many contexts but not to weekday names, where it is entirely neutral.
The example 星期四我有中文课 illustrates how days of the week function as topic-initial time frames: 星期四 (on Thursday) + Subject + Verb.
中文 (Zhōngwén) and 汉语 (Hànyǔ) are near-synonyms; 中文 emphasizes the written aspect while 汉语 emphasizes spoken language.

cards.spanishPhrase: El viernes salimos con los amigos.
cards.chinesePhrase: 星期五我们和朋友一起出去。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xīngqī wǔ wǒmen hé péngyou yīqǐ chūqù.
cards.grammarNote:
星期五 (xīngqī wǔ) is Friday, the fifth day.
The example 星期五我们和朋友一起出去 contains 出去 (chūqù, to go out), a directional compound: 出 (chū, exit) + 去 (qù, go away).
Chinese frequently uses such directional verb complements to specify the direction of movement.
Note that 出去 here stands alone without a stated destination, implying 'go out socializing' from context—Chinese often omits destinations that are understood from the situation.

cards.spanishPhrase: El sábado voy de compras.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xīngqī liù wǒ qù mǎi dōngxi.
cards.grammarNote:
星期六 (xīngqī liù) is Saturday, associated with weekend leisure in the modern work week.
The example 星期六我去买东西 uses the purposive serial verb construction: 去 (qù, go) + 买东西 (mǎi dōngxi, buy things), where the two verbs share one subject and the second expresses the purpose of the first.
东西 (dōngxi, things/stuff) literally means 'east-west,' a compound that historically referenced the east and west markets of Tang-dynasty Chang'an where goods were sold.
Both 星期日 and 星期天 (xīngqī tiān) are correct for Sunday. 星期天 is slightly more colloquial.
cards.spanishPhrase: El domingo descanso en casa.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xīngqī rì wǒ zài jiā xiūxi.

cards.spanishPhrase: Este fin de semana vamos al parque.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhège zhōumò wǒmen qù gōngyuán.
cards.grammarNote:
周末 (zhōumò) combines 周 (zhōu, week) + 末 (mò, end/tip), literally 'the end of the week.' 末 contrasts with 初 (chū, beginning): 周初 (zhōuchū, beginning of the week), 月末 (yuèmò, end of the month), 年末 (niánmò, year-end).
The measure word for weekend is 个: 这个周末 (zhège zhōumò, this weekend).
In modern Chinese, 周末 is more common than 星期末 (xīngqīmò), which sounds slightly awkward; 周末 is the standard colloquial form.

cards.spanishPhrase: Los días laborales me levanto temprano.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Gōngzuò rì wǒ hěn zǎo qǐchuáng.
cards.grammarNote:
工作日 (gōngzuò rì) literally means 'work day': 工作 (gōngzuò, work) + 日 (rì, day).
日 is the more literary/formal character for 'day,' compared to the colloquial 天 (tiān); it appears in formal compounds like 节日 (jiérì, holiday), 生日 (shēngrì, birthday), and 假日 (jiǎrì, day off).
An alternative term is 平日 (píngrì, ordinary days/weekdays) or 平时 (píngshí, usually/on normal days).
The example 工作日我很早起床 uses 起床 (qǐchuáng, to get up), a common morning routine phrase.
Season words use 天 or 季 suffix: 春天/春季, 夏天/夏季, etc. 天 is more colloquial; 季 is more formal or written.
cards.spanishPhrase: En primavera florecen muchas flores.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Chūntiān yǒu hěn duō huā kāi le.

cards.spanishPhrase: En verano hace mucho calor aquí.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Xiàtiān zhèlǐ hěn rè.
cards.grammarNote:
夏天 (xiàtiān) pairs 夏 (xià, summer) + 天 (tiān, sky/day/time of year).
The four seasons all use 天 as a suffix in colloquial speech: 春天 (chūntiān), 夏天, 秋天 (qiūtiān), 冬天 (dōngtiān); their formal equivalents use 季 (jì): 夏季 (xiàjì).
Weather adjectives in Chinese use the 很 (hěn) + adjective structure as a default predicate: 夏天这里很热 (xiàtiān zhèlǐ hěn rè, it's very hot here in summer).
Without 很, the bare adjective 热 implies comparison rather than description—so 很 is required even when no strong emphasis is intended.

cards.spanishPhrase: En otoño las hojas caen al suelo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qiūtiān shùyè luò xiàlái le.
cards.grammarNote:
秋天 (qiūtiān) combines 秋 (qiū, autumn) + 天 (tiān).
The character 秋 is composed of 禾 (hé, grain) + 火 (huǒ, fire), evoking the image of harvested grain drying under the autumn sun—a direct pictographic etymology.
The example 秋天树叶落下来了 uses the directional complement 落下来 (luò xialái, fall down), where 下来 (xialái) specifies downward movement toward the ground.
了 at the end marks a change of state (the leaves have now fallen).
Autumn is culturally associated with the Mid-Autumn Festival (中秋节 Zhōngqiū Jié).

cards.spanishPhrase: En invierno nieva mucho en las montañas.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Dōngtiān shān shàng xià hěn duō xuě.
cards.grammarNote:
冬天 (dōngtiān) pairs 冬 (dōng, winter) + 天 (tiān).
The character 冬 originally depicted a rope with two knots at each end, symbolizing the terminal point of the year.
The example 冬天山上下很多雪 uses the location word 山上 (shān shàng, on the mountain/mountaintop), where 上 is a localizer suffix.
The verb 下雪 (xià xuě, to snow) literally means 'snow falls down,' parallel to 下雨 (xià yǔ, to rain); both use 下 (xià, down) as a directional element.
天气 refers to current or short-term weather. 气候 (qìhòu) is used for long-term climate. Common question: 今天天气怎么样？(How's the weather today?)
cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Cómo está el tiempo hoy?
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān tiānqì zěnme yàng?

cards.spanishPhrase: Hoy es un día soleado y bonito.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān shì qíngtiān, hěn hǎokàn.
cards.grammarNote:
晴天 (qíngtiān) combines 晴 (qíng, clear/sunny) + 天 (tiān, day/sky).
The character 晴 contains the sun radical 日 (rì) on the left and 青 (qīng, blue-green) on the right, suggesting a clear blue sky.
Weather descriptions in Chinese follow the Subject + 是 + weather-noun pattern: 今天是晴天 (jīntiān shì qíngtiān, today is a sunny day), unlike English adjective predicates ('it is sunny').
The adjective form 天气很晴朗 (tiānqì hěn qínglǎng) uses the extended form 晴朗 for a more descriptive register.

cards.spanishPhrase: Hoy está nublado, puede llover.
cards.chinesePhrase: 今天是阴天，可能会下雨。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān shì yīntiān, kěnéng huì xià yǔ.
cards.grammarNote:
阴天 (yīntiān) combines 阴 (yīn, overcast/yin/shade) + 天 (tiān, day).
阴 is the feminine/dark/negative principle in the Taoist yin-yang (阴阳 yīnyáng) duality, and its association with shadow and clouds is conceptually fitting.
The example 今天是阴天，可能会下雨 uses 可能 (kěnéng, possibly) + 会 (huì, will/likely to) before the verb 下雨 (xià yǔ, to rain), a double-modal construction expressing possibility.
阴 contrasts with 晴 (qíng, clear/sunny) as antonyms in weather vocabulary.
下雨 is a verb phrase meaning 'to rain' (literally 'descend rain'). To say 'it's raining': 在下雨 or 下雨了. The noun 'rain' alone is 雨.
cards.spanishPhrase: Está lloviendo, lleva un paraguas.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zài xià yǔ, dài shàng nǐ de sǎn ba.

cards.grammarNote: 下雪 follows the same pattern as 下雨: verb phrase meaning 'to snow'. The noun 'snow' alone is 雪.
cards.spanishPhrase: Está nevando mucho afuera hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān wàimiàn xià le hěn duō xuě.

cards.grammarNote: 刮风 is a verb phrase meaning 'wind is blowing' (literally 'scrape wind'). The noun 'wind' alone is 风. Say: 今天刮风 (it's windy today).
cards.spanishPhrase: Hoy hace mucho viento, tengo frío.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān guā fēng, wǒ hěn lěng.

cards.grammarNote: 温度 is the general word for temperature. 气温 (qìwēn) specifically means air/atmospheric temperature in weather forecasts.
cards.spanishPhrase: La temperatura hoy es de veinte grados.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān de wēndù shì èrshí dù.

cards.grammarNote: The measure word for clouds is 朵 (duǒ): 一朵云 (one cloud). 朵 is also used for flowers.
cards.spanishPhrase: Hay muchas nubes en el cielo hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān tiān shàng yǒu hěn duō yún.

cards.grammarNote: 太阳 (sun) is different from 阳光 (yángguāng — sunlight/sunshine). To say 'the sun is shining': 太阳出来了 (the sun has come out).
cards.spanishPhrase: El sol brilla mucho esta tarde.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān xiàwǔ tàiyáng hěn liàng.

cards.spanishPhrase: El viento hoy es muy fuerte.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīntiān de fēng hěn dà.
cards.grammarNote:
风 (fēng) is a single-character noun meaning wind.
The standard pattern for describing wind strength is 风很大 (fēng hěn dà, the wind is very strong), where 大 (dà, big) is used for intensity rather than English 'strong.' The measure word for a gust or instance of wind is 阵 (zhèn): 一阵风 (yī zhèn fēng, a gust of wind).
风 appears in many compounds: 台风 (táifēng, typhoon), 风景 (fēngjǐng, scenery/landscape), and 风格 (fēnggé, style).

cards.spanishPhrase: Me gusta escuchar la lluvia por la noche.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xǐhuān wǎnshàng tīng yǔ shēng.
cards.grammarNote:
雨 (yǔ) is a pictograph of raindrops falling from a cloud.
As a noun, 雨 takes the measure word 场 (chǎng) for events or bouts: 一场雨 (yī chǎng yǔ, a rainfall/a rain event).
The verb 'to rain' is 下雨 (xià yǔ, literally 'rain descends'), a verb-object compound.
雨声 (yǔshēng, the sound of rain) in 我喜欢晚上听雨声 uses 声 (shēng, sound) as a noun-forming suffix.
雨 also appears in 梅雨 (méiyǔ, plum rains/monsoon season).

cards.spanishPhrase: La nieve en las montañas es muy bonita.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Shān shàng de xuě hěn hǎokàn.
cards.grammarNote:
雪 (xuě) pictographically shows rain (雨) above a broom-like 彐 stroke, evoking the act of sweeping away snow.
The verb for snowing follows the same pattern as rain: 下雪 (xià xuě, to snow), literally 'snow comes down.' To describe snow as a noun with a measure word, use 场 (chǎng): 一场雪 (yī chǎng xuě, a snowfall).
The example 山上的雪很好看 uses 好看 (hǎokàn, good-looking/beautiful), which literally means 'good to look at.' 雪 appears in 雪人 (xuěrén, snowman) and 雪山 (xuěshān, snow-capped mountain).
告诉 takes two objects: the person told and the information. Pattern: 告诉 + person + information. E.g., 我告诉他这件事 (I told him about it).
cards.spanishPhrase: Por favor, dime la verdad.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng gàosù wǒ zhēnxiàng.

cards.grammarNote: 问 can take a direct object (the person asked) or be followed by a question: 我问他几点了 (I asked him what time it was).
cards.spanishPhrase: Quiero preguntarte algo importante.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我想问你一个重要的问题。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiǎng wèn nǐ yī gè zhòngyào de wèntí.

cards.spanishPhrase: Por favor, responde mi pregunta.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng huídá wǒ de wèntí.
cards.grammarNote:
回答 (huídá) is a verb-object compound: 回 (huí, to return) + 答 (dá, to answer/reply), conveying the idea of returning a response.
It takes a direct object: 回答问题 (huídá wèntí, answer a question), 回答我 (huídá wǒ, answer me).
A near-synonym is 答复 (dáfù, to reply formally) or 回复 (huífù, to reply in writing/messages).
The example 请回答我的问题 uses 请 (qǐng, please) as a polite imperative marker before the verb—standard in requests.
聊天 is a verb-object compound. To say 'chat with someone': 跟...聊天. E.g., 我跟朋友聊天 (I chat with friends).
cards.spanishPhrase: A menudo charlo con mis amigos.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīngcháng gēn péngyou liáotiān.

cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Puedes explicarme este problema?
cards.chinesePhrase: 你能给我解释一下这个问题吗？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ néng gěi wǒ jiěshì yīxià zhège wèntí ma?
cards.grammarNote:
解释 (jiěshì) combines 解 (jiě, to untie/solve) + 释 (shì, to release/explain), together meaning 'to untie and release [understanding]'—a vivid metaphor for explaining.
It is a transitive verb taking a direct object or the pattern 给 + person + 解释: 给我解释一下 (gěi wǒ jiěshì yīxià, explain to me).
The 一下 (yīxià) suffix softens a request, suggesting a brief or casual action.
The example 你能给我解释一下这个问题吗？uses 能 (néng, can/able to) for polite capability questions.
介绍 can be reflexive (自我介绍, introduce oneself) or transitive (介绍朋友, introduce a friend).
cards.spanishPhrase: Quiero presentarte a mi amigo.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我想给你介绍我的朋友。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiǎng gěi nǐ jièshào wǒ de péngyou.

cards.spanishPhrase: Descríbeme cómo es tu casa.
cards.chinesePhrase: 请描述一下你家是什么样的。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Qǐng miáoshù yīxià nǐ jiā shì shénme yàng de.
cards.grammarNote:
描述 (miáoshù) pairs 描 (miáo, to trace/depict) + 述 (shù, to narrate/state), together meaning 'to trace out in words.' A near-synonym is 描写 (miáoxiě, to describe/portray, especially in writing or literature); 描述 is more neutral and factual, while 描写 has a literary connotation.
The example uses the pattern 请描述一下……是什么样的 (please describe what X is like), where 什么样的 (shénme yàng de, what kind of/what it is like) is the standard question form for descriptions.
讨论 typically takes 关于... (about) or a noun phrase as its object: 讨论这个问题 (discuss this problem).
cards.spanishPhrase: Vamos a discutir este tema juntos.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我们一起讨论这个问题吧。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒmen yīqǐ tǎolùn zhège wèntí ba.

cards.grammarNote: To agree with someone: 同意 + person / 同意某人的意见. To agree on something: 同意 + verb phrase.
cards.spanishPhrase: Estoy de acuerdo con tu opinión.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ tóngyì nǐ de yìjiàn.

cards.spanishPhrase: No estoy de acuerdo con esa idea.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ fǎnduì nàge xiǎngfǎ.
cards.grammarNote:
反对 (fǎnduì) is composed of 反 (fǎn, opposite/against) + 对 (duì, correct/facing), literally 'going against.' It functions as both a verb (我反对, I oppose) and a noun (表示反对, to express opposition).
The antonym is 支持 (zhīchí, to support).
In the example 我反对那个想法, 那个 (nà gè, that) uses 个 as the default classifier for abstract nouns like 想法 (xiǎngfǎ, idea/thought).
反 is a highly productive prefix: 反应 (fǎnyìng, reaction), 反复 (fǎnfù, repeatedly).
建议 can be a verb (suggest) or a noun (suggestion/recommendation). As a verb: 我建议你去 (I suggest you go).
cards.spanishPhrase: Te sugiero descansar un poco más.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jiànyì nǐ duō xiūxi yīxià.

cards.grammarNote: 打电话 is a verb-object compound. To call someone: 给...打电话 or 打电话给... E.g., 给我打电话 (call me).
cards.spanishPhrase: Te llamo por teléfono esta noche.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Jīn wǎn wǒ gěi nǐ dǎ diànhuà.

cards.grammarNote: 发短信 is a verb-object compound. To text someone: 给...发短信.
cards.spanishPhrase: Te envío un mensaje de texto ahora.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiànzài gěi nǐ fā duǎnxìn.

cards.grammarNote: 写信 is a verb-object compound. To write to someone: 给...写信.
cards.spanishPhrase: Quiero escribirle una carta a mi abuela.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiǎng gěi wǒ de nǎinai xiě xìn.

cards.grammarNote: 翻译 can be a verb or noun (translator). Direction is expressed with 把...翻译成...: 把它翻译成中文 (translate it into Chinese).
cards.spanishPhrase: ¿Puedes traducir esto al español?
cards.chinesePhrase: 你能把这个翻译成西班牙语吗？
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Nǐ néng bǎ zhège fānyì chéng Xībānyá yǔ ma?

cards.grammarNote: 想 has two uses: (1) to miss / to think about (想家, miss home), (2) modal verb 'want to / plan to' before a verb (我想去, I want to go).
cards.spanishPhrase: Quiero ir al cine esta noche.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ jīn wǎn xiǎng qù kàn diànyǐng.

cards.grammarNote: 知道 expresses knowing facts or information. For knowing/recognizing people or places, use 认识 instead.
cards.spanishPhrase: Sé que ella vive cerca de aquí.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ zhīdào tā zhù zài zhè fùjìn.

cards.grammarNote: 觉得 introduces a subjective feeling or opinion, often followed by a clause: 我觉得这很难 (I feel this is difficult).
cards.spanishPhrase: Siento que este trabajo es muy difícil.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ juéde zhège gōngzuò hěn nán.

cards.grammarNote: 认为 expresses a more reasoned opinion than 觉得. Often used in formal speech: 我认为你是对的 (I think you are right).
cards.spanishPhrase: Pienso que tienes razón.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ rènwéi nǐ shì duì de.

cards.grammarNote: 理解 implies deeper comprehension, often of abstract ideas. For understanding spoken language in context, 明白 or 听懂 may be more natural.
cards.spanishPhrase: No entiendo bien esta pregunta.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ bù tài lǐjiě zhège wèntí.

cards.spanishPhrase: Creo en lo que me dijiste.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ xiāngxìn nǐ shuō de huà.
cards.grammarNote:
相信 (xiāngxìn) combines 相 (xiāng, mutually/toward) + 信 (xìn, trust/believe/letter), conveying a sense of directing trust toward someone.
The object of 相信 is typically a person or a clause: 我相信你 (I believe you), 我相信你说的话 (I believe what you said), where 你说的话 (nǐ shuō de huà) uses the 的 (de) nominalization pattern to turn a verb phrase into a noun phrase.
相信 is stronger and more personal than 认为 (rènwéi, to think/consider), which expresses opinion rather than trust.
记得 means to still have a memory of something (retained memory). To memorize actively, use 记 or 记住 (resultative complement: successfully remembered).
cards.spanishPhrase: Recuerdo bien nuestro primer encuentro.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我很记得我们第一次见面。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ hěn jìde wǒmen dì yī cì jiànmiàn.

cards.grammarNote: 忘记 is the completed act of forgetting. 忘了 (with 了) is the colloquial form: 我忘了 (I forgot). 忘 alone can be used as a resultative complement: 忘掉 (forget completely).
cards.spanishPhrase: Olvidé traer mi paraguas hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ wàngjì dài sǎn le.

cards.grammarNote: 发现 can be followed by a noun or a clause: 我发现了一个问题 (I discovered a problem) / 我发现他不在 (I found out he wasn't there).
cards.spanishPhrase: Descubrí un restaurante muy bueno cerca.
cards.chinesePhrase: 我发现附近有一家很好的餐厅。
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ fāxiàn fùjìn yǒu yī jiā hěn hǎo de cāntīng.

cards.grammarNote: 注意 can function as a verb (to notice / to pay attention to) or as an imperative warning: 注意！(Watch out! / Be careful!).
cards.spanishPhrase: Nota que el semáforo está en rojo.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Zhùyì hóng dēng liàng le.

cards.grammarNote: 决定 can be followed by a noun or verb phrase: 我决定去北京 (I decided to go to Beijing).
cards.spanishPhrase: Decidí quedarme en casa hoy.
cards.chinesePhrasePin: Wǒ juédìng jīntiān dāi zài jiālǐ.

cards.grammarNote: 准备 also serves as a modal-like verb meaning 'to be about to / plan to': 我准备出发了 (I'm about to set off).
